alignments.rationale: The publication consolidates the detection quality improvement into a single statement that matches the preprint's numeric claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The robustness of eczema lesion detection improved by approximately 40% compared to the previous model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality and robustness of eczema lesion detection increased by approximately 25% and 40%, respectively, compared with that of the previous eczema detection model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same robustness improvement figure alongside the quality improvement, matching the preprint's claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the downstream severity prediction remained unchanged despite improvements in lesion detection quality and robustness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the downstream severity prediction remained unchanged. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result that downstream severity prediction performance did not change.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Use of skin segmentation as an alternative to eczema segmentation that requires specialist labeling showed performance on par with when eczema segmentation is used. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a new specific conclusion about skin segmentation as an alternative approach that was not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine-learning based decision support tools have been developed across many aspects of chronic kidney disease care. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning based decision support tools have been developed for various aspects of chronic kidney disease care. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Wording is effectively identical with no change in meaning, scope, or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Algorithms developed in the prediction and diagnosis of chronic kidney disease may help facilitate early disease prevention and assist with early planning of renal replacement therapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Algorithms for predicting and diagnosing chronic kidney disease may facilitate early disease prevention and assist with planning renal replacement therapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content and emphasis are the same with only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The systematic review aims to evaluate the application of prognostic and diagnostic machine-learning tools in chronic kidney disease development and progression. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The systematic review aims to evaluate the application of prognostic and diagnostic machine-learning tools in chronic kidney disease development and progression. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in purpose and framing between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This systematic review will offer valuable insight into the performance and reporting quality of machine-learning based models in chronic kidney disease diagnosis and prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This systematic review will provide insights into the performance and reporting quality of machine-learning models in chronic kidney disease diagnosis and prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same conclusion expressed with slightly different phrasing but no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mechanistic virtual patient modeling can be used to capture specific features of patients’ states and dynamics while reducing biases introduced by heterogeneous datasets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mechanistic virtual patient modeling can significantly reduce biases introduced by learning from heterogeneous datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the bias-reduction conclusion more directly and strongly ("significantly reduce") compared with the preprint's formulation of use while reducing bias.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More robust cluster configurations were observed in clustering using the VP model-based filtered data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: More robust cluster configurations were observed in clustering using the model-derived data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same result described with minor wording change (model-derived vs VP model-based filtered data).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VP model-based clustering reduced biases introduced by the inclusion of data from different hospitals and discovered an additional cluster with significant ARDS enrichment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: VP model-based clustering discovered an additional cluster with significant ARDS enrichment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the cluster-discovery result but omits the explicit statement about bias reduction contained in the preprint, narrowing the original combined claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results indicate that mechanistic VP modeling can significantly reduce biases introduced by learning from heterogeneous datasets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mechanistic virtual patient modeling can significantly reduce biases introduced by learning from heterogeneous datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication presents the bias-reduction conclusion more directly and with stronger certainty compared to the preprint's phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proportion of admissions with a positive diagnosis-based label was significantly higher for Stanford Peds compared to SickKids Peds across all outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proportion of admissions with a positive diagnosis-based label was significantly higher for StanfordPeds compared to SickKids across all outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical result across preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lab-based labels were more similar by institution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lab-based labels were more similar by institution. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in content and emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cohen’s Kappa and sensitivity were lower at SickKids Peds for all severity levels compared to Stanford Peds when using lab-based labels as the gold standard. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cohen’s Kappa and sensitivity were lower at SickKids for all severity levels compared to StanfordPeds when using lab-based labels as the gold standard. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same statistical comparison and conclusion reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diagnosis codes were consistently different between the two pediatric institutions across multiple outcomes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diagnosis codes were consistently different between the two pediatric institutions across multiple outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is unchanged but the publication reports a lower certainty level than the preprint, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The differences in diagnosis codes were not explained by differences in test results. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The differences in diagnosis codes were not explained by differences in test results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content remains the same but the publication presents it with lower certainty than the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among the ML models, the XGBoost model had the highest performance for fluid overload prediction with an AUROC of 0.78. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Among the ML models, the XGBoost model had the highest performance for fluid overload prediction with an AUROC of 0.78. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match of model performance and metric between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost model performed similarly to the final traditional logistic regression model with an AUROC of 0.70. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost model performed similarly to the final traditional logistic regression model with an AUROC of 0.70. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same comparative performance result reported in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Severity of illness scores and medication-related data were identified as the most important predictors of fluid overload. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Baseline severity of illness and ICU medication regimen complexity are important predictors of fluid overload. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Equivalent identification of key predictors with slightly different phrasing but same meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the context of this study, ML and traditional models appear to perform similarly to predict fluid overload in the ICU. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches may offer advantages over traditional regression techniques to predict fluid overload in the ICU. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's conclusion shifts the interpretation from 'similar performance' to suggesting possible advantages of ML, a substantive change in conclusion direction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After SBLTT, participants’ step lengths increased by 26 ± 13%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants' step lengths increased by 26 ± 13% after 24 sessions of Short Burst Locomotor Treadmill Training (SBLTT). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical quantitative result reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SBLTT directly increased step length for three participants by 13.5 ± 4.5%, while one participant decreased step length by −11.6%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using BART to control for speed or incline, step length increased for three participants (direct effect: 13.5 ± 4.5%), while one participant decreased step length (-11.6%). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is the same but the publication reports lower certainty and frames it with BART-specific adjustment, tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SBLTT had minimal effects on step length asymmetry and step width. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SBLTT had minimal effects on step length asymmetry and step width. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same negative/minimal-effect finding in both versions with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BART could accurately predict step length progression with an R² of 0.73 and plateaus in progression with an R² of 0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated datasets demonstrated that BART could accurately identify specified responses to training, including strong correlations for step length progression (R² = 0.73) and plateaus (R² = 0.87). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication clarifies that the R² results derive from simulated datasets, narrowing the context but preserving the reported metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work can help personalize rehabilitation and understand the causal mechanisms driving individual responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Causal modeling and Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) can be used to accurately track the direct effects of treadmill training on gait. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication focuses the general preprint claim on tracking direct effects via causal modeling/BART (narrower focus) and expresses the capability with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 43.0% of patients with diagnostic codes for PCC and COVID-19 were diagnosed with PCC less than 4 weeks after the initial recorded COVID-19 diagnostic code.
alignments.pub_claim_text: 61.3% of patients with diagnostic codes for PCC and COVID-19 were diagnosed with PCC less than 12 weeks after initial recorded COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different percentage using a longer time window (12 weeks vs 4 weeks), so the original result has been revised and the temporal scope broadened, while the strength/confidence of reporting remains similar.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The high-performing tree-based model showed that patient demographics, practice visited, clinician visited, and calendar date of visit were more predictive of PCC diagnostic code than any symptom.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinicians in the top 1% of diagnostic propensity accounted for more than a third of all PCC diagnoses (35.8%).
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes and quantifies the effect of clinicians specifically (top 1% driving diagnoses), which clarifies and narrows the broader preprint claim about multiple non-symptom predictors toward a clinician-centric explanation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inter-rater agreement on PCC diagnosis was moderate, with a Cohen’s kappa of 0.60.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is substantial disagreement between clinicians on diagnostic criteria for PCC.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding qualitatively (substantial disagreement) rather than giving the numeric kappa; this is a clarification of the same result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the natural language classifiers was poor, with the best F1 score being 0.54.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the natural language classifiers was marginal with a best AUC of 0.724.
alignments.rationale: Both texts report weak classifier performance but the publication uses a different performance metric and value (AUC instead of F1), so the quantitative claim is revised while the overall conclusion (marginal performance) remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiple sources of heterogeneity in the documentation of PCC diagnostic codes in primary care practices have created challenges for public health surveillance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variation in diagnostic rates across clinicians points to the possibilities of under- and over-diagnosis for patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's broader concern about heterogeneity as concrete variation across clinicians leading to under- and over-diagnosis, which clarifies the implications for surveillance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-level-cadence method (2LC) showed the smallest minimum moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) duration associated with 50% of optimal risk reduction compared to the one-level-cadence method (1LC) and machine learning (ML) methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-level-cadence-method (2LC) exhibited the most pronounced association with all-cause and CVD mortality at the lower end of the duration spectrum.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same finding about 2LC being strongest at low durations but frames it for both all-cause and CVD mortality (broader outcome focus), thus clarifying and slightly broadening the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning (ML) method elicited the greatest mortality risk reduction for vigorous physical activity (VPA) compared to the two-level-cadence method (2LC) and one-level-cadence method (1LC).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning (ML) method elicited the greatest mortality risk reduction compared to other methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative result about the ML method producing the largest mortality risk reduction; wording and emphasis are essentially unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After standardizing duration, the machine learning (ML) method exhibited the most pronounced associations with mortality compared to the two-level-cadence method (2LC) and one-level-cadence method (1LC).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ML method provided the most pronounced association with all-cause and CVD mortality, suggesting it may be appropriate for estimating health benefits of moderate and vigorous intensity physical activity in observational studies.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core result but adds an interpretive recommendation about appropriateness for estimating health benefits, which amplifies the implication of the original standardized-duration finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-level-cadence method (2LC) exhibited the most pronounced association with mortality at the lower end of the duration.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-level-cadence-method (2LC) exhibited the most pronounced association with all-cause and CVD mortality at the lower end of the duration spectrum.
alignments.rationale: Both claims state the same observation about 2LC and low durations; the publication frames it as a high-confidence result across outcomes, which strengthens (amplifies) the finding.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The standardized minimum durations in the moderate physical activity (MPA)-CVD mortality association were highest for the ML method.
alignments.rationale: This publication detail about standardized minimum durations for MPA-CVD being highest for ML is not present in the preprint and appears as an added specific result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Chronic migraine (CM) patients showed an increased Brain Age Gap compared to healthy controls (4.16 vs -0.56 years, P=0.01).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Chronic migraine (CM) patients showed an increased Brain Age Gap compared to healthy controls (4.16 vs -0.56 years, P=0.01).
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identical quantitative result for chronic migraine, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Episodic migraine (EM) patients exhibited a smaller Brain Age Gap compared to healthy controls, although it did not reach statistical significance (1.21 vs -0.56 years, P=0.19).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Episodic migraine (EM) patients exhibited a smaller Brain Age Gap that did not reach statistical significance (1.21 vs -0.56 years, P=0.19).
alignments.rationale: The episodic migraine result is reproduced verbatim in the publication, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain imaging features previously associated with migraine were among the main drivers of the differences in the predicted age.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Brain imaging features previously associated with migraine were among the main drivers of the differences in the predicted age.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion about imaging features driving predicted age differences is identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The brain-predicted age has shown to be a sensitive biomarker of chronic migraine patients and can help reveal distinct aging patterns in migraine.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The brain-predicted age has shown to be a sensitive biomarker of chronic migraine patients and can help reveal distinct aging patterns in migraine.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the biomarker conclusion unchanged.

alignments.pub_claim_text: No associations were found between the Brain Age Gap and headache or migraine frequency, or duration of the disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports an additional null-finding relating Brain Age Gap to clinical frequency/duration that was not present in the preprint, so this is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The derived CBC model shows an area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) of 0.872 for predicting sepsis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CBC model shows an area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) of 0.872.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the exact AUROC result for the CBC model unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External validations show AUROCs of 0.805 and 0.845 for MIMIC-IV.
alignments.pub_claim_text: External validations show AUROCs of 0.805 for University Medicine Greifswald and 0.845 for MIMIC-IV.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model including PCT revealed a significantly higher performance than PCT alone.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model including PCT revealed a significantly higher AUROC than PCT alone.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same comparative performance between the combined model and PCT alone with unchanged meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Routine CBC results could significantly improve diagnosis of sepsis when combined with ML.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Routine CBC results could significantly improve diagnosis of sepsis when combined with ML.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about CBC plus ML improving sepsis diagnosis is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CBC model can facilitate early sepsis prediction in non-ICU patients with high robustness in external validations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CBC model can facilitate early sepsis prediction in non-ICU patients with high robustness in external validations.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same practical conclusion about early sepsis prediction and robustness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A temporal attention model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0·71 ± 0·01, indicating good discrimination performance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.71 ± 0.01, indicating good discrimination performance.
alignments.rationale: The performance metric and interpretation are the same in both versions; wording is equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The history of disability progression is more predictive for future disability progression than treatment or relapses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The history of disability progression is more predictive for future disability progression than treatment or relapses history.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same predictor hierarchy and wording, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Good discrimination and calibration performance on an external validation set was achieved using only routinely collected variables.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Good discrimination and calibration performance on an external validation set is achieved using only routinely collected variables.
alignments.rationale: The claim about external validation performance using routine variables is reproduced unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed models are ready for a clinical impact study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning models can reliably inform clinicians about the future occurrence of progression.
alignments.rationale: The publication moves from a cautious readiness-for-impact-study statement to a stronger claim that models can reliably inform clinicians, broadening the practical implication and amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The digital phenotyping algorithm developed can accurately reconstruct pregnancy journeys and normalize them across different hospital EMR systems. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The digital phenotyping algorithm developed accurately reconstructed pregnancy journeys and normalized them across different hospital EMR systems. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with essentially identical wording and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models constructed using machine learning approaches predicted preeclampsia with high accuracy, achieving AUCs of 0.92, 0.83, and 0.89 across ante-, intra-, and postpartum periods, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive models constructed using machine learning approaches predicted preeclampsia with high accuracy, achieving AUCs of 0.92, 0.82, and 0.89 at 37 gestational weeks, intrapartum, and postpartum, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same core AUC results but adds specificity (37 weeks/intrapartum) and slightly adjusts one numeric value (0.83→0.82), so the claim is clarified rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach identified both known and novel risk factors for preeclampsia, including complete blood count related characteristics and ibuprofen usage. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach identified both known and potential new risk factors for preeclampsia, including complete blood count related characteristics for the antepartum period. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens 'novel' to 'potential new', omits ibuprofen, and specifies the antepartum period, representing a revision that narrows the claim and slightly tempers confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of the model substantially exceeds what is achieved today in clinical practice, promoting personalized precision therapeutic strategies for patients at risk. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model provides a path for promoting personalized precision therapeutic strategies for patients at risk of preeclampsia. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication couches the original strong claim about exceeding current practice into a more guarded statement about providing a path toward personalized strategies and lowers certainty, so the claim is revised and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-stage super learner has better performance compared with a one-stage super learner and individual algorithms for healthcare cost estimation under a wide variety of settings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-stage super learner demonstrates better performance compared to a one-stage super learner and individual algorithms for healthcare cost estimation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative performance claim with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The improvement of the two-stage super learner over the one-stage super learner is particularly evident in settings when zero-inflation is high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The improvement of the two-stage super learner over the one-stage super learner is particularly evident in settings where zero-inflation is high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this specific result verbatim, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-stage super learner provides researchers an effective approach for healthcare cost analyses in environments where they cannot know the best single algorithm a priori. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed two-stage super learner is well suited for healthcare expenditure data by separately estimating the probability of any healthcare expenditure and the mean amount of healthcare expenditure conditional on having healthcare expenditures. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's recommendation into a methodological description explaining why the approach is suitable, clarifying the mechanism while retaining the same conclusion about suitability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel 5-plex PCR assay developed for detection of carbapenem-resistant genes has a lower limit of detection of 10 DNA copies per reaction for each primer set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel 5-plex PCR assay developed for detection of five major carbapenem-resistant genes has a lower limit of detection of 10 DNA copies per reaction for each primer set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same limit-of-detection result with matching detail; the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AMCA classifier demonstrated excellent predictive performance with 99.6% accuracy, representing a 7.9% increase compared to conventional melting curve analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AMCA classifier demonstrated excellent predictive performance with 99.6% accuracy, representing a 7.9% increase compared to conventional melting curve analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance metrics and comparative improvement, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining the AMCA method with the multiplex assay increases classification accuracy of detecting major carbapenem-resistant genes in clinical CPO-isolates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining the AMCA methodology with the multiplex assay improves classification accuracy by utilizing real-time data from a single-fluorescent channel. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion about improved accuracy but adds the mechanism (real-time, single-channel), so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AMCA method can enhance the throughput and performance of molecular diagnostic platforms without requiring hardware modifications or additional costs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AMCA method increases the throughput and performance of molecular diagnostic platforms without hardware modifications and additional costs. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same claim about throughput/performance and lack of hardware/cost needs but reports higher certainty, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 68.1% of participants were willing to donate their own organs, while only 51.8% were willing to donate a relative’s organs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 68.1% of participants were willing to donate their own organs, while only 51.8% were willing to donate a relative's organs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same descriptive statistics and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Consistency in organ donation decisions was predicted by religion and fears about organ donation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Consistency in willingness to donate was predicted by religion and fears about organ donation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same predictors but reports higher certainty, so the semantic content is unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants expressed high confidence in their decision-making abilities despite 78% having never discussed organ donation with their relatives. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals were overconfident in their decision-making abilities regarding organ donation, with 78% having never discussed it with their relatives yet expressing high confidence in respecting their wishes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same observation explicitly as 'overconfidence' and emphasizes respecting relatives' wishes; wording sharpens the interpretation and certainty is elevated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlights the distinct psychological processes involved when donation decisions are made for family members. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Addressing the decision-maker's mindset, such as overconfidence and the use of heuristics, may be key to increasing organ donation rates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication moves from describing psychological processes to recommending targeting specific mindsets (overconfidence, heuristics); this clarifies actionable focus but lowers stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Addressing the decision-maker’s mindset may be key to actualizing potential donors identified in intensive care units. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Addressing the decision-maker's mindset, such as overconfidence and the use of heuristics, may be key to increasing organ donation rates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the recommendation to address mindset but broadens the target from ICUs to overall donation rates and provides examples (overconfidence, heuristics), clarifying and broadening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimized multimodal fusion machine-learning model performed better than the clinically recommended linear-regression MRE model in assessing ileal Crohn's disease endoscopic activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized fusion model performed better than the clinically recommended model as indicated by a better median test MSE distribution and a better aggregated AUC over the folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core comparative result and adds specific evaluation metrics (MSE, aggregated AUC), clarifying the evidence for superior performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The median test mean-squared-error distribution of the optimized fusion model was significantly lower than that of the clinically recommended model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized fusion model performed better than the clinically recommended model as indicated by a better median test MSE distribution and a better aggregated AUC over the folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly cites the median test MSE distribution (and additional AUC evidence) as support; the original MSE-focused claim is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Length of disease segment and normalized biochemical biomarkers were identified as the most informative features for model development. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Length of disease segment and normalized biochemical biomarkers were identified as the most informative features for model development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same feature-importance finding but reports higher certainty, so the claim content is unchanged with amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optimized machine-learning models have the potential to enable accurate and non-invasive observation of intestinal inflammation in Crohn's disease patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Optimized ML models for ileal CD endoscopic activity assessment have the potential to enable accurate and non-invasive attentive observation of intestinal inflammation in CD patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion about potential for accurate, non-invasive assessment using optimized ML models with comparable certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models demonstrated very good overall sensitivity (0.89-0.94) and specificity (0.87-0.89) for the classification of ASD vs. non-ASD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning models demonstrated very good overall sensitivity (0.89-0.94) and specificity (0.87-0.89) for predicting ASD diagnoses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance metrics and claim are the same between preprint and publication with only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models showed increases of up to 20% in sensitivity or specificity for individuals with less severe symptoms around the ADOS cut-off. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models showed increases of up to 35% in sensitivity or specificity for individuals with less severe symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a larger maximum improvement (35% vs 20%) and higher certainty, changing the magnitude and confidence of the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distinct patterns of importance for specific ADOS-items were revealed in the analysis of the ANX-, ADHD-, and CD-models compared to the unspecific model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distinct patterns of importance for specific ADOS items were revealed when comparing the ANX, ADHD, and CD models with the unspecific model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content and level of certainty are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using machine learning-based diagnostic classification may improve clinical decisions by utilizing the full range of information from comprehensive diagnostic observation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ML-based diagnostic classification may improve clinical decisions by utilizing the full range of information from detailed diagnostic observation instruments. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines wording (detailed diagnostic observation instruments) but the substantive conclusion remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This strategy might be particularly relevant for individuals with less severe symptoms, who typically present a difficult decision for clinicians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This strategy might be particularly relevant for older children with less severe symptoms, where diagnostic decisions are often difficult. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds an age qualifier (older children), narrowing the target subgroup while keeping the same conclusion about relevance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 16 informative clinical variables for predicting Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, motor neuron disease, and myasthenia gravis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A panel of informative diagnostic biomarkers for predicting Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, motor neuron disease, and myasthenia gravis was identified. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the finding as a panel of biomarkers (slightly different wording) but conveys the same identification result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multinomial model could correctly predict the diagnosis of one of the four neurodegenerative diseases with an accuracy of 88.3%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A multinomial model trained on accessible clinical markers could correctly predict a neurological disease diagnosis with an accuracy of 88.3%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Accuracy and claim are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found evidence for shared genetic risk loci across neurodegenerative diseases, including SNPs in cancer-related genes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is evidence for shared genetic risk loci among neurological diseases, including SNPs in cancer-related genes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same evidence for shared loci and the mention of cancer-related SNPs matches the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study highlights the potential for hypothesis-free mathematical modelling of easily measured clinical variables to identify diagnostic biomarkers for neurodegenerative diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work demonstrates the value of data-driven approaches to identify novel biomarkers in the absence of any known or promising biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication expresses the same conclusion about data-driven approaches but with stronger emphasis and higher stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our analysis supports current knowledge regarding the ageing-related degeneration/cancer shift. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or reiterate the ageing-related degeneration/cancer shift claim present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Single nucleotide polymorphisms implicated in several craniofacial disorders were identified in a genome-wide association study of neurological disease patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This GWAS finding (craniofacial-associated SNPs) does not appear in the preprint and is introduced newly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A donor-specific epigenetic classifier was developed to reduce the incidence of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) in allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A donor-specific epigenetic classifier was developed to reduce the incidence of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) by improving donor selection. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the intended use (improving donor selection) but preserves the core contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier achieved an area under the ROC curve (AUC) of 0.91 in the discovery cohort, indicating strong predictive capability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The initial classifier demonstrated an encouraging area under the ROC curve (AUC) of 0.91 in the discovery cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric result and interpretation are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Validation of the classifier in an independent cohort resulted in a significantly lower AUC of 0.51, suggesting that donor DNA methylation may not be a suitable predictor of aGVHD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Validation of the classifier in an independent cohort resulted in a significantly lower AUC of 0.51, indicating failure to validate its predictive capability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication report the same validation failure and AUC result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlights the importance of independent validation of machine learning classifiers for clinical use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study highlights the importance of independent validation of machine learning classifiers intended for clinical use. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is the same but the publication assigns higher certainty to the importance of independent validation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Twelve proteins were predicted by machine learning as candidate diagnosis biomarkers for colon cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Twelve proteins were predicted by machine learning as colon cancer candidate diagnosis biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The prediction of twelve candidate proteins is stated the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CHGA protein showed the best diagnostic accuracy among the candidate protein biomarkers with an AUC of 0.9 in microarray data and 0.995 in RNA-seq data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CHGA protein demonstrated the best diagnostic accuracy with an AUC of 0.9 in microarray data and 0.995 in RNA-seq data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric AUC results and ranking of CHGA are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The loss of CHGA protein expression in normal colorectal mucosa may serve as a potential candidate biomarker for colon or colorectal cancer diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Immunohistochemistry examination confirmed the bioinformatical prediction of CHGA as a potential biomarker for early diagnosis of colon cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds confirmatory IHC evidence (increasing confidence) and narrows the claim toward early colon cancer diagnosis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predicted biomarkers showed close relationships with reported biomarkers on the protein-protein interaction network and shared some pathways. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the PPI/network relationships claim present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: XGBoost was more accurate in identifying individuals who later suffered a myocardial infarction compared to logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGBoost was more accurate in identifying individuals who later suffered a myocardial infarction compared to logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core comparative accuracy claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ROC score for XGBoost was 0.86, while for logistic regression it was 0.77. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGBoost outperformed the logistic regression model in receiver operator characteristic (ROC) scores, with scores of 0.86 compared to 0.77. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The ROC results and interpretation are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SHAPley values can be used to visualize and interpret the predictions made by XGBoost models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SHAPley values can be used to visualize and interpret the predictions made by XGBoost models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The methodological claim is the same but the publication reports it with lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost machine learning model shows very promising results in evaluating risk of myocardial infarction in a large and diverse population. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost machine learning model shows very promising results in evaluating risk of myocardial infarction in a large and diverse population. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The overall contribution/conclusion is retained with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Both models were equally precise, but the regression model classified more of the healthy class correctly. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This nuance about precision and class-specific performance is not present in the preprint and appears as a new result in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Published risk scores to predict severe complications from Clostridioides difficile infection demonstrate poor performance upon external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report that existing published risk scores perform poorly; this claim from the preprint is not represented in the paper, so it appears removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A prediction model based on structured electronic health record data accurately estimates risk for complications from Clostridioides difficile infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A prediction model was developed and validated that estimates risk for complications from Clostridioides difficile infection with good accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that a model using EHR data was developed and validated with good accuracy, matching the preprint claim closely.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models using structured electronic health records can accurately predict risk of severe complications related to Clostridioides difficile infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A prediction model was developed and validated that estimates risk for complications from Clostridioides difficile infection with good accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication describes development and validation of ML-based prediction from EHR data consistent with the preprint's general claim about ML models' predictive ability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lasso regression, random forest, and stacked ensemble models all performed well with AUC ranging between 0.89-0.9.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All three machine learning models demonstrated good performance with an area under the receiver operating curve (AUC) of 0.88-0.89.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report good performance of the three models, but the AUC range changed slightly between preprint and publication, so this is a small numerical revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Elastic Net machine learning model achieved the best area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.851, which is 7.0% higher than the logistic regression model's AUC of 0.795.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ML model Elastic Net (EN) achieved the best AUC of 0.851, which is 7.0% higher than the logistic regression model's AUC of 0.795.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric AUC comparison and interpretation as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the Elastic Net model were 88.2% and 65.9%, respectively, compared to 73.0% and 72.8% for the logistic regression model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the Elastic Net model were 88.2% and 65.9%, respectively, compared to 73.0% and 72.8% for the logistic regression model.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance metrics are reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning combined with feature selection improves the prediction accuracy of diabetic kidney disease risk in an asymptomatic stable population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning, along with feature selection, improves prediction accuracy of diabetic kidney disease risk in an asymptomatic stable population.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about ML plus feature selection improving prediction accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified novel risk factors for diabetic kidney disease, including metabolites related to lipids, lipoproteins, fatty acids, and ketone bodies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified novel risk factors for diabetic kidney disease, including metabolites related to lipids, lipoproteins, fatty acids, and ketone bodies.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same identified novel risk factors as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DNNs demonstrated high performance in detecting no ERM, small ERM, and large ERM cases with AUC values of 0.99, 0.92, and 0.99, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DNNs demonstrated high performance in detecting no-ERM, small-ERM, and large-ERM cases with AUC values of 0.99, 0.92, and 0.99, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUC values and performance for the DNNs as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Small ERMs were identified as the most difficult to detect among the different sizes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study indicates that small-ERMs are the most difficult to detect among the different sizes of ERMs.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same conclusion that small ERMs are hardest to detect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Saliency maps reliably highlighted ERMs regardless of the presence of other OCT features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Saliency maps generated by the DNNs reliably highlighted ERM, even in the presence of other OCT features.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same finding about saliency maps reliably highlighting ERMs despite other OCT features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DNNs can reliably detect and grade ERMs according to their size in both the fovea and the paracentral region.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep-neural-networks (DNNs) can reliably detect and grade epiretinal membranes (ERM) according to their size in both the fovea and paracentral regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overall conclusion about reliable detection and grading by DNNs in the same retinal regions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model could be used for screening programs or decision support systems in the future.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model could be utilized for future screening programs or decision-support systems.
alignments.rationale: Both claim future utility, but the publication expresses this with somewhat higher certainty (medium vs low), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with airway stenosis have a higher risk of airway failure and require additional precautions before medical interventions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with airway stenosis have a higher risk of airway failure and require additional precautions before medical interventions.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same clinical conclusion as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stenosis and stridor are often misdiagnosed as other respiratory conditions, leading to worsening outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stenosis and stridor are often misdiagnosed as other respiratory conditions, leading to worsening outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same statement about misdiagnosis and outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Customized transformer-based models achieved AUC scores of 0.875 for stenosis detection and 0.864 for stridor detection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Customized transformer-based models achieved AUC scores of 0.875 for stenosis detection and 0.864 for stridor detection.
alignments.rationale: The numeric model performance metrics are preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed methods have potential to add value as screening tools in real-world clinical workflows, particularly in emergency departments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed methods have the potential to serve as valuable screening tools in real-world clinical workflows, especially in emergency departments.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same potential application of the methods in clinical workflows and emergency settings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adaptive DBS approach (aDBS) modulates stimulation based on the power in the beta range of STN local field potential.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not mention aDBS modulation based on STN beta power; this specific claim from the preprint is absent in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LAURA, a Transformer-based framework, predicts the nonlinear evolution of subthalamic beta power up to six days in advance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LAURA, a Transformer-based framework, predicts the nonlinear evolution of subthalamic beta power up to 6 days in advance.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same predictive capability and time horizon for LAURA as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High prediction accuracy greater than 90% was achieved in four PD patients with chronic DBS over months of recordings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High prediction accuracy (>90%) was achieved in four PD patients with chronic DBS over months of recordings.
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms the same >90% accuracy in the same patient cohort and recording duration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study paves the way for remote monitoring strategies and the implementation of new algorithms for personalized auto-tuning aDBS devices.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study paves the way for remote monitoring strategies and the implementation of new algorithms for personalized auto-tuning aDBS devices.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same conclusion regarding implications for remote monitoring and personalized auto-tuning algorithms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our CNN achieved state-of-the-art performance with 0.50 F1-score. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our convolutional neural network (CNN) achieved a 0.50 F1-score on the annotated Twitter corpus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric result and model description with the same certainty; there is no substantive change in meaning or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Non-adherence is evident in a subset of tweets with nine categories of reasons for non-adherence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Nonadherence to medication is evident in a subset of tweets, which can be categorized into 9 reasons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding (presence of nonadherence and nine categories) with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Social media users publicly discuss medication treatment changes and may explain their reasons including when it constitutes non-adherence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Social media users publicly discuss medication treatment changes and may explain their reasons for nonadherence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about public discussion of treatment changes and reasons for nonadherence with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using social media data may address the limitations of current methods to identify medication non-adherence. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using social media data may address the limitations of current methods for identifying medication nonadherence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same proposed contribution (social media can address existing method limitations) with equivalent framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The rVERDICT intracellular volume fraction f ic discriminated between Gleason 3+3 and 3+4 with a p-value of 0.003. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rVERDICT intracellular volume fraction fic discriminated between Gleason 3 + 3 and 3 + 4 (p = 0.003) and Gleason 3 + 4 and ≥ 4 + 3 (p = 0.040), outperforming classic VERDICT and the ADC from mp-MRI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the original 3+3 vs 3+4 result but adds additional discrimination (3+4 vs ≥4+3) and performance context; this clarifies and broadens the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: rVERDICT achieved higher accuracy, F1-score, and Cohen’s kappa compared to classic VERDICT and ADC from mp-MRI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rVERDICT intracellular volume fraction fic discriminated between Gleason 3 + 3 and 3 + 4 (p = 0.003) and Gleason 3 + 4 and ≥ 4 + 3 (p = 0.040), outperforming classic VERDICT and the ADC from mp-MRI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states that rVERDICT outperformed classic VERDICT and ADC, matching the preprint performance claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The repeatability of rVERDICT parameters was high, with R² values ranging from 0.79 to 0.98. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: rVERDICT parameters exhibited high repeatability when rescanning five patients (R2 = 0.79–0.98; CV = 1–7%; ICC = 92–98%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms the same R² repeatability range and provides additional repeatability metrics (CV, ICC), clarifying the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: T2 values estimated with rVERDICT were not significantly different from those estimated with an independent multi-TE acquisition. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rVERDICT T2 values are not significantly different from those estimated with the independent multi-TE acquisition (p > 0.05). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same non-significant difference but with formal p-value reporting and increased certainty, which amplifies the confidence in the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: rVERDICT allows for accurate, fast, and repeatable estimation of diffusion and relaxation properties of prostate cancer. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rVERDICT model allows for accurate, fast and repeatable estimation of diffusion and relaxation properties of PCa sensitive enough to discriminate Gleason grades 3 + 3, 3 + 4 and ≥ 4 + 3. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the original conclusion about accuracy, speed, and repeatability but clarifies and broadens it by explicitly adding sensitivity to discriminate multiple Gleason grades.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network models demonstrated better discriminative power and calibration compared to linear logistic ridge regression models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The neural network models demonstrated better discriminative power and calibration compared to linear logistic ridge regression models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative performance finding with identical framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The main prediction drivers for inappropriate dosing included the use of anti-inflammatory, antidiabetic, and anti-Parkinson’s drugs, as well as a diagnosis of chronic kidney failure. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The main prediction drivers for inappropriate dosing included the use of anti-inflammatory, antidiabetic, and anti-Parkinson's drugs, as well as a diagnosis of chronic kidney failure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same set of main predictors but with higher stated certainty, amplifying confidence in the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our models can flag patients at high risk of receiving at least one inappropriate dose daily in a controlled in-silico setting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our models can flag patients at high risk of receiving at least one inappropriate dose daily in a controlled in-silico setting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this conclusion verbatim and with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A prospective clinical study may confirm that the model's predictions hold in real-life settings and translate into benefits in hard endpoints. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A prospective clinical study may confirm that the model's predictions hold in real-life settings and translate into benefits in hard endpoints. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same recommendation for prospective clinical validation with unchanged caution and wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform demonstrates 86.82% accuracy in detecting the likely presence of Pulmonary Tuberculosis (PTB). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform demonstrates 86.82% accuracy in detecting the likely presence of pulmonary tuberculosis (PTB). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the exact accuracy result and model claim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model shows 90.36% sensitivity and 84.67% specificity in detecting PTB. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model shows 90.36% sensitivity and 84.67% specificity in detecting PTB. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sensitivity and specificity values are identical between preprint and publication; the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pilot testing of the model resulted in a Positive Predictive Value of 75% for PTB positive cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The positive predictive value of the model in pilot testing was 75% for presumptive PTB cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same PPV observed in pilot testing with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform has the potential to provide a cost-effective PTB screening method in resource-poor areas. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform has the potential to provide a cost-effective PTB screening method in resource-poor areas. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same potential application claim with similar caution and framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified several neural networks, including the Default Mode Network (DMN), Frontoparietal Network (FPN), Dorsal Attention Network (DAN), and Visual Network (VN), that distinguish between Mild Cognitive Impairment (PD-MCI) and PD dementia (PDD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several neural networks, including the default mode network (DMN), frontoparietal network (FPN), dorsal attention network, and visual network (VN), are significant in differentiating between PD-MCI and PDD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same set of networks and the specific differentiation between PD-MCI and PDD with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Sensory Motor Network (SMN), Visual Network (VN), Default Mode Network (DMN), and Salience Network (SN) are important for discriminating PD patients with no cognitive impairment (PD-NC) from those with PD dementia (PDD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensory motor network, VN, DMN, and salience network (SN) are important for discriminating PD-NC from PDD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication mirrors the preprint's result listing the same networks relevant for PD-NC vs PDD discrimination.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Compared to healthy controls, the Default Mode Network (DMN), Frontoparietal Network (FPN), Visual Network (VN), and Salience Network (SN) are significant networks for the differential diagnosis of cognitive impairment subtypes in Parkinson's disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Resting-state networks (RSN) can be a discriminating factor in distinguishing the CI stages of PD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the preprint's specific network findings into a broader conclusion that RSNs can discriminate cognitive impairment stages; this clarifies and broadens the original specific comparison to healthy controls.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resting state networks can be utilized to stratify the cognitive impairment subtypes of Parkinson's disease patients in clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Resting-state networks (RSN) can be a discriminating factor in distinguishing the CI stages of PD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint recommends clinical utilization of RSNs for stratification, while the publication presents a more cautious, general conclusion that RSNs can discriminate stages, narrowing the claimed translational application and tempering clinical implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Universal IHC (UIHC) analyzer outperforms conventional single-cohort models in interpreting unseen IHCs, achieving a Kappa score of 0.578 compared to up to 0.509. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Universal IHC (UIHC) analyzer outperformed conventional single-cohort models in analyzing unseen IHC images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same outperforming result but omits the specific Kappa values; the core claim is preserved though less numerically specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The UIHC model consistently shows superior performance across different positive staining cutoff values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The UIHC model demonstrated consistent performance across varying positive staining cutoff values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and meaning are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Qualitative analysis reveals that UIHC effectively clusters patches based on expression levels. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the qualitative clustering claim about patch-level clustering/expression; this preprint result does not appear in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The UIHC model quantitatively assesses c-MET expression with MET mutations, representing a significant advancement in AI application in personalized medicine. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The UIHC model assigned higher tumor proportion scores to MET amplification cases compared to other non-small cell lung cancer cases. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a related quantitative assessment for MET amplification cases but differs from the preprint's phrasing about MET mutations and presents a more limited/altered focus and lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Psychiatric disorders are important comorbid disorders of long-term cognitive change. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anxiety and depression are important longitudinal predictors of cognitive change. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the general statement about psychiatric disorders to the specific disorders anxiety and depression, preserving the central claim about predictive importance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Anxiety and depression are stronger predictors of cognitive change than cardiovascular disease and diabetes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Psychiatric disorders are more important comorbidities of long-term cognitive change than diabetes and cardiovascular disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys essentially the same comparative claim but with slightly reduced certainty (medium vs high).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning methods provide more robust and accurate prediction models for longitudinal cognitive data. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly assert a general superiority or robustness claim for machine learning methods; no matching publication claim is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Poor mental health has important predictive effects on longitudinal cognitive decline. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Poor mental health should be considered in secondary and primary care for its predictive effects on longitudinal cognitive decline. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the predictive-effect claim but frames it as a recommendation for clinical consideration and reports it with somewhat lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine Learning models can accurately predict intensive care admission requirements with a 5-day area under the receiver operator characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) of 0.986. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operator characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) for predicting n-day risk of ICU admission ranges from 0.981 to 0.995. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports ROC-AUC performance as an n-day range that includes the preprint's 5-day value, providing a broader numeric context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The need for ventilator support can be predicted with a ROC-AUC of 0.995 using Machine Learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operator characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) for predicting n-day risk of ICU admission ranges from 0.981 to 0.995. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's reported ROC-AUC range includes the ventilator prediction value and frames it within n-day predictive performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 10-day predictions of ICU resource requirements using Machine Learning models are comparable to 5-day predictions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of forecasting models decreases when predicting many days into the future. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a decrease in performance for longer horizons, which contradicts the preprint's claim of comparability for 10-day vs 5-day predictions and thus revises the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random Forest-based modelling can be used for accurate forecasting of ICU resource requirements during the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Random Forest-based modelling can be used for accurate n-day forecasting predictions of ICU resource requirements, when n is not too large. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion about RF modelling but adds the explicit limitation that accuracy holds when forecasting horizons are not excessively long.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The GBDT-SHAP pipeline identified 193 potential risk factors from 11,639 predictors, with 60% of the variable importance being directly health related. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning pipeline identified 193 potential risk factors from 11,639 predictors using SHAP values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the identification of 193 predictors from 11,639 using SHAP, matching the preprint's result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cox models adjusted for baseline characteristics showed evidence for an association with mortality for 166 out of the 193 predictors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cox models adjusted for baseline characteristics showed evidence for an association with mortality for 166 out of the 193 predictors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The adjusted Cox model result is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pipeline was able to detect associations in the presence of interactions and non-linear relationships. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GBDT-SHAP pipeline effectively identified relevant predictors hidden within thousands of variables. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's contribution statement captures the pipeline's ability to find relevant predictors despite complex relationships, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nineteen predictors showed evidence for an association in unadjusted analyses but not in adjusted analyses, suggesting bias by confounding. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For 19 predictors, evidence for an association with mortality was observed in unadjusted analyses but not in adjusted analyses, indicating potential bias by confounding. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same observation about the 19 predictors and confounding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The GBDT-SHAP pipeline provides a fast and pragmatic solution for the first stage of hypothesis-free risk factor identification. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GBDT-SHAP pipeline effectively identified relevant predictors hidden within thousands of variables. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same contribution of the pipeline as an effective, pragmatic first-stage tool for identifying predictors; meaning is preserved though phrasing differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models for diabetic kidney disease progression achieved an accuracy of 0.95 with clinical risk factors and improved to an accuracy of 0.98 when omics-based predictors were included. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicts the progression to diabetic kidney disease with an accuracy of 0.96 ± 0.25 and 0.96 ± 0.06 area under curve using clinical measurements and small molecule predictors respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different numeric performance values and uncertainty estimates than the preprint; the core claim (high predictive performance) remains but the stated metrics are revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: During a median follow-up of 5.4 years, 21% of participants progressed in diabetic kidney disease and 29% progressed to diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not report these incidence/progression percentages, so this specific descriptive result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models for diabetic retinopathy progression were less predictive, achieving an AUROC of 0.81 with clinical risk predictors and 0.87 with omics included. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For diabetic retinopathy, the model achieves an area under curve of 0.75 ± 0.14 and 0.79 ± 0.16 with clinical measurements and small molecule predictors respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (lower and uncertainty-expressed) AUC values for retinopathy than the preprint, so the numeric result was revised while the general conclusion of lower performance remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study demonstrates the application of machine learning to effectively predict five-year progression of complications, particularly diabetic kidney disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning tools could facilitate the implementation of precision diagnosis in the clinic with further validation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
