alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicting the change in FEV 1 over time is more challenging than simply predicting the future absolute FEV 1 level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicting the change in FEV<sub>1</sub> over time is more challenging than simply predicting the future absolute FEV<sub>1</sub> level.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion verbatim but assigns higher certainty (published result), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model performance accuracy was best for GOLD1-2 subjects and it was harder to achieve accurate prediction in advanced stages of the disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of the prediction model was best for Global initiative for chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) grades 1-2 participants.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same finding but presents it with slightly lower reported certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This prediction model facilitates the identification of smokers at increased risk for rapid disease progression.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This prediction model facilitates the identification of smokers at increased risk for rapid disease progression.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint conclusion directly with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant room for improvement in future models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is significant room for improvement in future models for predicting FEV<sub>1</sub> progression.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication state the same limitation about room for improvement with similar certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Random forest provided slightly better performance than linear regression in predicting FEV<sub>1</sub> progression.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a model-comparison result (random forest vs linear regression) not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A random forest model using polygenic risk scores from ADHD and genetically correlated phenotypes, along with an optimized group of 20 gene set polygenic risk scores, achieved an area under the receiving operating characteristic curve of 0.72.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiving operating characteristic curve (AUC) for a random forest model using polygenic risk scores (PRSs) and gene set polygenic risk scores (gsPRSs) was 0.72, indicating improved prediction accuracy for ADHD.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same model AUC and interpretation as the preprint without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the receiving operating characteristic curve of 0.72 represents a statistically significant improvement over logistic regression models and random forest models using only polygenic risk scores from ADHD and genetically correlated phenotypes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AUC of 0.72 was a statistically significant improvement over logistic regression models and random forest models using only PRS from ADHD and genetically correlated phenotypes.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same statistical-comparison result as in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Summing risk at the gene set level and incorporating genetic risk from disorders with high genetic correlations with ADHD improved the accuracy of predicting ADHD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Summing risk at the gene set level and incorporating genetic risk from disorders with high genetic correlations with ADHD improved the accuracy of predicting ADHD.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same conclusion but reports it with slightly lower certainty (medium vs high in preprint), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Learning curves suggest that additional improvements in predictive accuracy would be expected with larger study sizes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Learning curves suggest that additional improvements in prediction accuracy would be expected with larger study sizes.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state this expectation, but the publication rates the certainty lower (low), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Better accounting of genetic risk and the genetic context of allelic differences results in more predictive models for ADHD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Better accounting of genetic risk and the genetic context of allelic differences results in more predictive models for ADHD.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conceptual conclusion but with higher stated certainty (medium vs low), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hierarchical ensemble classification models can predict severe outcomes for hospitalized COVID-19 patients using readily available information at hospital admission.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning-based models can assess the risk of severe outcomes for patients hospitalized with COVID-19 infection using attributes routinely collected at hospital admission.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint conclusion in equivalent terms (ML models using admission attributes to assess risk) without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tests achieved precision in the lowest risk groups near or above 0.9 during both development and validation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tests achieved precision in the lowest risk groups near or above 0.9 during both development and validation.
alignments.rationale: The precision result is repeated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proportion of patients with severe outcomes significantly increased across increasing risk groups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proportion of patients with severe outcomes significantly increased across increasing risk groups.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same graded-risk finding with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CRP, LDH, and D-dimer were often found to be important attributes in the assignment of risk label for COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: C-reactive protein, lactate dehydrogenase, and D-dimer were often found to be important in the assignment of risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same important biomarkers and does not alter the claim's meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Risk of severe outcomes for patients hospitalized with COVID-19 infection can be assessed using machine learning-based models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning-based models can assess the risk of severe outcomes for patients hospitalized with COVID-19 infection using attributes routinely collected at hospital admission.
alignments.rationale: This preprint conclusion is essentially identical to the publication's main conclusion and is matched to the same publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An electronic frailty index developed using machine learning can improve short-term mortality prediction in patients with heart failure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An electronic frailty index developed using machine learning can improve short-term mortality prediction in patients with heart failure.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the preprint's main conclusion about the electronic frailty index without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PNI, age, and NLR were the most important variables predicting 30-day and 90-day mortality in heart failure patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prognostic nutritional index, age, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio were the most important variables predicting 30 day and 90 day mortality.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same top predictors with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gradient boosting significantly outperformed decision tree and multivariate logistic regression in predicting mortality outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gradient boosting significantly outperformed decision tree and multivariable logistic regression in predicting mortality.
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms the algorithm performance comparison as stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The electronic frailty index based on comorbidities, inflammation, and nutrition information can readily predict mortality outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the curve for gradient boosting was 0.90 for predicting 30 day mortality and 0.92 for predicting 90 day mortality.
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies the predictive performance (AUCs) underlying the preprint's general claim that the index can predict mortality, thereby clarifying the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Exploratory analyses demonstrated that clinical features differed substantially between severe and non-severe COVID-19 types.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly restate the specific result that clinical features differed substantially between severe and non-severe cases; related conclusions are reframed rather than directly reporting this exploratory finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Random forest models using comorbidity/symptom and biochemistry features achieved predictive accuracies of over 90% and 95%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest models achieved over 90% predictive accuracy using clinical results and over 95% using laboratory results independently.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance split (clinical/comorbidity vs laboratory/biochemistry) with essentially identical accuracy figures and framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining the top 10 multimodal features, the random forest model achieved over 99% predictive accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using the top 10 multimodal features as input, the random forest model achieved 97% predictive accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The published result gives a lower accuracy (97% vs >99%), representing a substantive numerical revision and a tempered claim about model performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings provide insights on effectively evaluating COVID-19 patient's severity and developing treatment plans accordingly.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides insights on evaluating the disease severity of COVID-19 patients based on common medical features when gold standard features are unavailable.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's broader claim into a more specific context (evaluating severity when gold standard features are unavailable), removing explicit mention of developing treatment plans.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Symptoms and comorbidities can be used as an initial screening tool for triaging COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinical information can be used as an initial screening tool for self-evaluation and triage in COVID-19 patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core claim that clinical features can be used for screening/triage but extends the application to self-evaluation and slightly rephrases the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study developed a machine learning model with 84% prediction accuracy for moderate to severe respiratory failure in COVID-19 pneumonia patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study developed a machine learning model with 84% prediction accuracy for moderate to severe respiratory failure in hospitalized COVID-19 pneumonia patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the qualifier 'hospitalized' making the population more explicit; the reported accuracy and main claim remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A fourth 'boosted mixed model' achieved the best predictive performance with an AUC of 0.84 without worsening the false negative rate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A fourth 'boosted mixed model' achieved the best predictive performance with an AUC of 0.84 without worsening the false negative rate.
alignments.rationale: The claim is reproduced verbatim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models were built using a hybrid approach that combined clinical expertise with data-driven analysis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive models were built using a hybrid approach that combined clinical expertise with data-driven analysis.
alignments.rationale: No change in content or framing between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model can assist clinicians in the decision-making process and contribute to the development of new analytics for improved care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model can assist clinicians in the decision-making process and contribute to the development of new analytics for improved care.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.854.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.854.
alignments.rationale: The reported AUC is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Application of the model to an independent patient dataset resulted in a comparable AUC of 0.838, validating its generalization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Application of the model to an independent patient dataset resulted in a comparable AUC of 0.838, validating its generalization.
alignments.rationale: The external-validation result is reported unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicted initial SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR positivity in 66% of individuals whose RT-PCR result changed from negative to positive within two days.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicted initial SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR positivity in 66% of individuals whose RT-PCR result changed from negative to positive within 2 days.
alignments.rationale: The numerical result and phrasing are consistent (only minor formatting change 'two days'→'2 days').

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This model offers opportunities for early and rapid identification of high-risk SARS-CoV-2 infected patients before their RT-PCR results are available.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This model offers opportunities for early and rapid identification of high-risk SARS-CoV-2 infected patients before their RT-PCR results are available.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about early identification is carried forward without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning analysis of MRI data has shown potential in predicting response for individual patients with major depressive disorder (MDD).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning analysis of MRI data has shown potential in predicting response for individual patients with major depressive disorder (MDD).
alignments.rationale: The textual claim is the same but the publication assigns a lower certainty (medium) compared with the preprint (high), indicating a tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The meta-analysis of twenty-two samples including 957 patients showed an overall area under the bivariate summary receiver operating curve of 0.84.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Meta-analysis of 22 samples including 957 patients showed an overall area under the bivariate summary receiver operating curve of 0.84.
alignments.rationale: The numeric result and meaning are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification performance was higher for electroconvulsive therapy outcome prediction than for medication outcome prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification performance was higher for electroconvulsive therapy outcome prediction than medication outcome prediction.
alignments.rationale: The claim content is unchanged, but the publication presents this result with higher certainty (high vs medium), representing amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prediction of treatment response using machine learning analysis of MRI data is promising but should not yet be implemented into clinical practice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction of treatment response using machine learning analysis of MRI data is promising but should not yet be implemented into clinical practice.
alignments.rationale: The cautionary conclusion is preserved verbatim with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future studies with more generalizable samples and external validation are needed to establish the potential of MRI to realize individualized patient care in MDD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future studies with more generalizable samples and external validation are needed to establish the potential of MRI to realize individualized patient care in MDD.
alignments.rationale: The limitation and call for future work are carried forward unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for the complete OSR dataset ranged from 0.83 to 0.90.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for the complete OSR dataset ranged from 0.83 to 0.90.
alignments.rationale: The reported AUC range is identical in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning (ML) can be applied to blood tests as an alternative method to rRT-PCR for identifying COVID-19-positive patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning (ML) can be applied to blood tests as an alternative method to rRT-PCR for the identification of COVID-19-positive patients.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is reproduced with only minor wording differences; meaning and scope are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The validations of the ML models achieved AUC from 0.75 to 0.78 and specificity from 0.92 to 0.96.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The validations of the ML models achieved good results, with AUC from 0.75 to 0.78 and specificity from 0.92 to 0.96.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a qualitative descriptor ('good results') but preserves the reported validation metrics; this is a minor clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hematochemical values of routine blood exams could represent a faster and less expensive alternative to the rRT-PCR test.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hematochemical values of routine blood exams could represent a faster and less expensive alternative to the rRT-PCR test.
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is carried forward identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model was trained using 35,447 attendances by 28,945 patients and evaluated on a hold-out test set featuring 8,847 attendances by 7,237 patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims list does not include the dataset size / training-test split detail present in the preprint claims, so this specific descriptive claim appears omitted from the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: On the hold-out test set, our highest performing model obtained an AUROC of 0.747 and an average precision of 0.233.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest performing model achieved an AUROC of 0.747 and an average precision of 0.233 on the hold-out test set.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric performance metrics on the hold-out set verbatim, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results demonstrate that machine-learning models can be used to classify patients, with moderate performance, into low and high-risk groups for reattendance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning models can classify patients into low and high-risk groups for reattendance with moderate performance.
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication lists this with lower certainty (medium vs high), so the claim content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We explained our models predictions using SHAP values, capable of explaining predictions at an attendance level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SHAP values were used to explain the model's predictions at an attendance level.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same use of SHAP but with higher certainty; the explanatory method is the same, so semantic content is unchanged while expressed confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated how clustering techniques can be used to investigate the different sub-groups of explanations present in our patient cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clustering techniques, specifically the UMAP algorithm, can investigate different sub-groups of explanations present in the patient cohort.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the clustering-findings but specifies the algorithm (UMAP), which clarifies the original claim without changing scope or expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Healthcare-based disease monitoring systems in France currently provide influenza activity information that lags real-time by 1 to 3 weeks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Healthcare-based disease monitoring systems in France currently provide influenza activity information that lags real-time by one to three weeks.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding about the one-to-three week lag in surveillance data with identical meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine-learning modeling approach can produce real-time estimates and short-term forecasts of influenza activity in France.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine-learning modeling approach can produce real-time estimates and short-term forecasts of influenza activity in France.
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated identically in the publication, so semantic content, scope and confidence are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All data sources contribute to improving influenza surveillance, and machine-learning ensembles that combine these sources lead to accurate and timely predictions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: All data sources contribute to improving influenza surveillance.
alignments.rationale: The preprint combined two assertions (all sources contribute; ensembles improve accuracy); the publication separates them. This mapping links the all-sources clause to Q3 while the ensemble-specific claim appears separately in the publication (see added claim).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Accurate real-time monitoring systems of influenza outbreaks could be useful for public health decisions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The temporal data gap in disease monitoring introduces uncertainty that prevents public health officials from having a timely perspective on population-level disease activity.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the utility claim as a stronger statement about the consequences of the data gap for public health decision-making, thereby clarifying and amplifying the policy relevance of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High Resolution Magic Angle Spinning Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (HRMAS NMR) technique can distinguish healthy and malign tissue efficiently using peak intensities of biomarker metabolites.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High Resolution Magic Angle Spinning Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (HRMAS NMR) technique can efficiently distinguish healthy and malignant tissue using peak intensities of biomarker metabolites.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about HRMAS NMR distinguishing healthy vs malignant tissue using metabolite peaks, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A random forest based approach can distinguish samples with tumor cells and controls accurately and effectively with a mean AUC of 85.6% and AUPR of 93.4%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A random forest based approach can accurately distinguish samples with tumor cells from controls with a median AUC of 85.6% and AUPR of 93.4%.
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics are preserved but the summary statistic changed from mean to median, so the claim has been revised (statistically) though scope and confidence remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We can further distinguish benign and malignant samples with a mean AUC of 87.1% and AUPR of 96.1%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method can further distinguish benign and malignant samples with a median AUC of 87.1% and AUPR of 96.1%.
alignments.rationale: As above, the substantive result is retained but the reported central tendency changed from mean to median, so this is a numerical revision without scope change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Known malignancy biomarkers such as creatine and 2-hydroxyglutarate play an important role in distinguishing tumor and normal cells and suggest new biomarker regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Known malignancy biomarkers such as creatine and 2-hydroxyglutarate play an important role in distinguishing tumor and normal cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the role of known biomarkers but omits the preprint's additional claim about suggesting new biomarker regions, narrowing the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We present a decision tree-based machine learning model for predicting in-hospital mortality and critical events in COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost classifier outperformed baseline models in predicting in-hospital mortality and critical events for COVID-19 patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the exact algorithm (XGBoost) and performance relative to baselines, clarifying the preprint's more generic 'decision tree-based' description while preserving the same contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieves strong performance, notably predicting mortality at 1 week with an AUC-ROC of 0.84.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) for mortality prediction using XGBoost was 0.89 at 3 days and 0.88 in external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (higher) AUCs at different time horizons (3 days and external validation) rather than the preprint's 1-week AUC, so the performance claim is revised and emphasizes stronger short-term/external results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identify key contributors in outcome prediction, including age, inflammatory markers, and coagulation parameters.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Acute kidney injury on admission, elevated LDH, tachypnea, and hyperglycemia were identified as the strongest drivers of critical event prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists a different, more specific set of strongest predictors than the preprint's broader grouping, indicating a substantive revision of which features were most important.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This is one of the first models with external validation to predict outcomes in COVID-19 patients with strong validation performance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning models developed in this study can effectively identify at-risk COVID-19 patients and predict their outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts emphasis from novelty/external validation to an assertion of effectiveness in identifying at-risk patients; this clarifies the practical takeaway and expresses stronger confidence in effectiveness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are four major clusters among the countries based on the cumulative number of confirmed COVID-19 cases and deaths.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis revealed that there are four major clusters among the countries affected by COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication report the same clustering result (four major clusters), so this claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The first principal component explains 60% of the total variation in COVID-19 data and is characterized by all variables except the rate variables.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claim set does not include the principal-component-level statement about explaining 60% of variation characterized by non-rate variables; instead the publication emphasizes cluster composition, so this specific PC-centric claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The number of tests conducted by a country is an unimportant variable for predicting the cumulative number of confirmed COVID-19 cases at a global level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variable number of tests by the country did not play a vital role in the prediction of the cumulative number of confirmed cases.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding that number of tests was not a vital predictor, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The remaining countries contribute to explaining 20% of the total variation in COVID-19 data through the second principal component, which is characterized by only three rate variables.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The remaining countries explain only 20% of the total variation characterized by rate variables.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result (remaining countries account for ~20% explained variation characterized by rate variables), so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DHRP clinics showed a more distinct reduction in hospital admission and day rates compared to control clinics by the end of the follow-up period in 2018. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DHRP clinics showed a more distinct reduction in hospital admission and day rates compared to control clinics by the end of the follow-up period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Texts and meaning are effectively identical between preprint and publication, aside from a minor wording of the follow-up period.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The change in the between group difference in hospital admission and day rates from baseline to the end of the follow-up was statistically significant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The change in the between group difference in hospital admission and day rates from baseline to the end of the follow-up was statistically significant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and statistical assertion are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ML model-based risk-directed interdisciplinary team interventions are associated with lower hospitalization rates and hospital day rates in HD patients compared to controls. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ML model-based risk-directed interdisciplinary team interventions are associated with lower hospitalization rates and hospital day rates in HD patients compared to controls. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is stated identically in the publication with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Strong correlation was observed between 25 Hz and 100 Hz sampling rates in overall activity measurement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Strong correlation was observed between 25- and 100-Hz sampling rates in overall activity measurement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Essentially identical claim about strong correlation between sampling rates.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lower overall acceleration was consistently observed in data collected at 25 Hz compared to 100 Hz. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Consistently lower activity was observed in data collected at 25 Hz compared to 100 Hz. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same observation of consistently lower activity/acceleration at 25 Hz; wording differs minimally.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Excellent agreement between sampling rates was observed in all machine learning classified activities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reduced sleep and light activity and increased sedentary time was classified in 25-Hz data by machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Preprint asserted excellent agreement across classified activities, whereas the publication reports specific systematic classification differences (reduced sleep/light activity, increased sedentary) indicating the earlier claim was softened and narrowed to specific activity classes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sampling rate should be consistently reported in physical activity studies and tailored to the outcome of interest. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sampling rate and resampling methods should be consistently reported in physical activity studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the recommendation to report sampling rate but clarifies and broadens it to explicitly include resampling methods, slightly shifting emphasis from tailoring to outcomes to consistent methodological reporting.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bariatric surgery induces significant shifts in the gut microbiota that could contribute to weight loss and metabolic benefits. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bariatric surgery induces significant shifts in the gut microbiota that could contribute to weight loss and metabolic benefits. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: High-level conclusion is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified a common microbial signature associated with Roux-en-Y Gastric bypass surgery that can discriminate pre- and post-surgical samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches demonstrated that the gut microbiota signature associated with RYGB surgery can discriminate between pre- and post-surgical samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication separates identification and the discriminatory demonstration; this preprint claim maps best to the publication claim about discrimination by ML and is substantively preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a striking similarity in fecal microbiota composition and metabolic pathways early after surgery across multiple datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A common microbial signature was identified following Roux-en-Y Gastric bypass surgery across multiple datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report a reproducible, common post-surgery microbial signature across datasets; wording differences are minor.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Opportunistic pathogen abundance consistently increased post-surgery across cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Opportunistic pathogen abundance increased post-surgery in a consistent manner across cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about increased opportunistic pathogens post-surgery is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Characterization of the microbial signature could provide insights into microbiome-based interventions for improving outcomes following bariatric surgery. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Characterization of the robust microbial signature could provide insights into microbiome-based interventions for improving outcomes following bariatric surgery. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The proposed utility of characterizing the signature is the same, but the publication downgrades the certainty (medium -> low), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A COVID-19 high-risk laboratory test result profile (COVID19-HRP) was identified, consisting of 21 routine blood tests that characterize SARS-CoV-2 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A COVID-19 high-risk laboratory test result profile (COVID19-HRP) was identified, consisting of 21 routine blood tests that characterized SARS-CoV-2 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identification and composition of the COVID19-HRP are stated the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Approximately half of the SARS-CoV-2 positive patients had the distinct COVID19-HRP that separated them from SARS-CoV-2 negative patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Approximately half of the SARS-CoV-2 positive patients had the distinct COVID19-HRP that separated them from SARS-CoV-2 negative patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Proportion and discriminatory aspect of the profile are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 patients with the COVID19-HRP had higher SARS-CoV-2 viral loads and poorer clinical outcomes compared to other positive patients without COVID19-HRP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 patients with the COVID19-HRP had higher SARS-CoV-2 viral loads and poorer clinical outcomes compared to other positive patients without the COVID19-HRP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Association of the profile with higher viral loads and worse outcomes is consistently reported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The percentage of SARS-CoV-2 patients with the COVID19-HRP has significantly decreased from March/April to May/June. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The percentage of SARS-CoV-2 patients with the COVID19-HRP significantly decreased from March/April to May/June. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The trend result is the same, but the publication reports a lower certainty (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This analysis could become an important tool in COVID-19 population disease severity tracking and prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This analysis could become an important tool in COVID-19 population disease severity tracking and prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusive recommendation for utility in tracking/prediction is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methodology can significantly reduce the potential CVD risk through personalized lifestyle modifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The personalized lifestyle modification recommended by the proposed methodology has the potential to effectively reduce the CVD risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the same outcome-oriented claim with slightly different phrasing emphasizing potential/effectiveness; substantive content is preserved but wording is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A classification-based prediction model accurately predicts CVD risk based on the condition of risk factors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A classification-based prediction model is implemented to predict the CVD risk based on the condition of risk factors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Implementation and purpose of the classification-based prediction model are the same between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generative adversarial network (GAN) learns the underlying relationship between risk factors and quantifies the uncertainty of disease progression under lifestyle modifications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generative adversarial network (GAN) is incorporated to learn the underlying relationship between risk factors and quantify the uncertainty of disease progression. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The role of the GAN is described consistently, but the publication reports lower certainty about this contribution (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel personalized exponential utility function model is proposed to evaluate the utilities of modifications with respect to CVD risk reduction. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A novel personalized exponential utility function model is proposed to evaluate the modifications' utilities with respect to CVD risk reduction. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The novel utility model claim is preserved, but the publication reduces the certainty from high to medium, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification accuracy achieved using the Multiple Kernel Learning classifier with the whole dataset was 83%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MKL classifier demonstrated a classification accuracy of 83% using the whole dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same numeric result for MKL accuracy with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Applying the Boruta feature selection algorithm yielded a classification accuracy of 86%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification accuracy improved to 86% after applying the Boruta feature selection method. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same post-Boruta accuracy improvement with the same numeric value and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study indicates that the differences between schizophrenic patients and healthy controls are robust across different classifiers. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts robustness across different classifiers; the paper does not reproduce this generalization, so the preprint claim is not present in the published set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variables that contributed most to the classification were mainly related to the latency and amplitude of the auditory P300. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variables that contributed most to the classification were mainly related to the latency and amplitude of the auditory P300 paradigm. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim conveys the same result but frames it with 'paradigm' and lower certainty, so the finding is clarified and the confidence is slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrated that Multiple Kernel Learning can be useful in distinguishing between schizophrenic patients and controls. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study showed that MKL can be useful in distinguishing between schizophrenic patients and controls when using ERP measures. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion about MKL usefulness but adds the context 'when using ERP measures', narrowing the scope while keeping confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ARISE score could be computed for 31,586 patients, identifying 2,376 (7.5%) patients with a high probability of elevated Lp(a). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ARISE score could be computed for 31,586 patients, identifying 2,376 patients with a high probability of elevated Lp(a). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same sample size and identified count with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A positive ARISE score was associated with significantly higher odds of elevated Lp(a) in the YNHHS (OR 1.87, 95% CI, 1.65-2.12) and the VUMC (OR 1.41, 95% CI, 1.24-1.60). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A positive ARISE score was associated with significantly higher odds of elevated Lp(a) in both the Yale-New Haven Health System and the Vanderbilt University Medical Center. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim restates the association across both health systems; numerical ORs are represented equivalently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate the feasibility of deployment of ARISE in US health systems to define the risk of elevated Lp(a), enabling a high-yield testing strategy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate the feasibility of deployment of ARISE in US health systems to define the risk of elevated Lp(a), enabling a high-yield testing strategy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this implementation feasibility conclusion verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We confirm the very low adoption of Lp(a) testing, which is also being restricted to a highly selected population. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study confirms the markedly low adoption of Lp(a) testing, which is being restricted to a highly selected population. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claim affirms the same conclusion about low adoption and selective testing with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean rate of kidney function decline in the general population was estimated to be -0.89 ml/min/1.73 m²/year over 10 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: There are no publication claims provided; this preprint result does not appear in the published claim set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost model demonstrated better predictive performance for rapid kidney function decline compared to the linear regression model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding publication claims exist to substantiate or repeat this model-comparison result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance metrics for the XGBoost model were RMSE of 0.78, R² of 0.35, and AUROC of 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Specific predictive metrics reported in the preprint are not present among the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The linear regression model using only eGFR data resulted in poor predictive performance with RMSE of 1.94, R² of -3.03, and AUROC of 0.79. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: These reported poor-performance metrics for the linear model are not represented in the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Random Forest ML model outperformed the TIMI score in predicting in-hospital mortality in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest ML model outperformed the TIMI score in predicting in-hospital mortality in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about in-hospital mortality prediction with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Random Forest ML model demonstrated better performance than the TIMI score for predicting 30-day mortality in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest ML model demonstrated better performance than the TIMI score for predicting 30-day mortality in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same 30-day mortality performance comparison without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Key predictors for worse outcomes at 30-days included mitral regurgitation on presentation, smoking, cardiogenic shock, diabetes, ventricular septal rupture, Killip class, age, female gender, low blood pressure, and low ejection fraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Key predictors for worse outcomes at 30-days included mitral regurgitation on presentation, smoking, cardiogenic shock, diabetes, ventricular septal rupture, Killip class, age, female gender, low blood pressure, and low ejection fraction. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The set of predictors is the same, but the publication reports this with lower stated certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model serves as a superior risk stratification tool compared to the traditional regression-based TIMI score in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model serves as a superior risk stratification tool compared to the traditional regression-based TIMI score in patients with STEMI. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion that the ML model is a superior risk stratification tool without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models can forecast the number of daily acute ischemic stroke admissions based on locoregional weather systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models using locoregional weather data can forecast daily acute ischemic stroke admissions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates that ML models using local weather data can forecast daily stroke admissions, matching the preprint conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGB model demonstrated the best test performance with the lowest mean absolute error of 1.21 cases per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGB performed best among the evaluated models with a mean absolute error of 1.21 cases per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication report XGB as best with the same MAE value and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Temperature extremes and stormy conditions significantly increase stroke incidences with distinct seasonal associations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cold and heat stressor days, as well as stormy conditions, increase stroke admissions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same relationship more specifically as 'cold and heat stressor days' and stormy conditions, clarifying terminology while preserving the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Maximum air pressure was identified as the top predictive variable for acute ischemic stroke admissions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Maximum air pressure is the top predictor of acute ischemic stroke admissions, with temperature showing a bimodal link. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication agrees that maximum air pressure is the top predictor and additionally notes a bimodal temperature link; the core claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By integrating the clinicopathological features and somatically mutated genes of patients, we achieved the highest accuracy; %93 for LUAD and %89 for LUSC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Integrating clinical features and somatically mutated genes achieved the highest accuracy in risk classification, with 93% for LUAD and 89% for LUSC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with identical accuracy numbers and variables, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: New prognostic genes such as KEAP1 for LUAD and CSMD3 for LUSC are significantly associated with the risk stratification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: New prognostic genes such as KEAP1 for LUAD and CSMD3 for LUSC are significantly associated with risk stratification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same gene-level finding with equivalent phrasing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed feature set allows clinicians and patients to assess the risk group according to patient-specific clinical and molecular parameters. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed machine learning model and mobile application allow clinicians and patients to assess survival risk using individual clinical and molecular feature sets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the deliverable (machine learning model and mobile app) and frames the assessment as survival risk, but preserves the core function and user group of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A user-friendly web interface is being implemented for clinicians and lung cancer patients to predict the risk stratification of individuals. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed machine learning model and mobile application allow clinicians and patients to assess survival risk using individual clinical and molecular feature sets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint stated an interface was being implemented; the publication presents a developed app (more concrete), so the claim is clarified and the practical confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine Learning methods can effectively predict smoking initiation with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning methods can accurately predict smoking initiation among adults using the PATH study data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert effective/accurate prediction of smoking initiation using the same dataset; wording differs but the claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified about 60 informative PATH variables that predict smoking initiation among adult never smokers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified about 60 informative PATH variables that predict smoking initiation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same (≈60 informative variables) but the publication lowers the stated certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three significant predictors of smoking initiation identified are BMI, dental/oral health status, and taking anti-inflammatory or pain medication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: BMI and dental and oral health status are important predictors of smoking initiation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains BMI and dental/oral health as predictors but omits the medication predictor, narrowing and revising the original set; overall certainty is reduced for the broader original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification models achieved a high discriminatory power with an area under the Specificity-Sensitivity curves of around 80%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning methods can accurately predict smoking initiation among adults using the PATH study data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a general accuracy conclusion rather than the numeric AUC; the core claim of good predictive performance is maintained but the metric is not reiterated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A digital insole measuring osteoarthritis-specific gait signatures yields similar results to the clinical gait-lab standard. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A digital insole measuring osteoarthritis-specific gait signatures yields similar results to the clinical gait-lab standard. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result verbatim, so there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model constructed in this work is highly predictive of force plate data from both a validation set and a separate, independent digital insole dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model constructed in this work was highly predictive of force plate data from both a validation set and a separate, independent digital insole dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same model performance claim with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Digital insole derived gait characteristics are comparable to traditional gait measurements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Digital insole-derived gait characteristics are comparable to traditional gait measurements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is essentially identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Individuals using digital insoles can be identified by their gait characteristics, even if they are healthy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals using digital insoles can be identified by their gait characteristics based on a single stride of raw sensor time-series data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that identification can be done from a single stride and raises certainty to high, clarifying and amplifying the original observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work provides a framework for a promising alternative to traditional clinical gait analysis methods. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work provides a framework for a promising alternative to traditional clinical gait analysis methods. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed IDSF algorithm delivers superior performance based on the area under the curve (AUC) score. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed information decomposition and selective fusion (IDSF) algorithm delivers superior performance based on the area under the curve (AUC) score. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the algorithm performance claim with the same metric and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study recommends the left anterior limb of internal capsule in diffusion MRI (dMRI) and the right middle temporal gyrus in functional MRI (fMRI) as potential biomarkers for predicting post-traumatic epilepsy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study recommends two magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) abnormalities as potential biomarkers for predicting post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE): the left anterior limb of internal capsule in diffusion MRI (dMRI) and the right middle temporal gyrus in functional MRI (fMRI). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same biomarker recommendations with identical anatomical and modality details.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A supervised binary classification task was created to contrast data from late post-traumatic seizure patients with those without late post-traumatic seizure. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A supervised binary classification task was created to contrast data from late post-traumatic seizure (LPTS) patients with those without LPTS. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the methodological contribution with the same framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT demonstrated high performance in accuracy, clarity, completeness, clinical utility, and overall rating according to expert evaluation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT shows high overall performance when evaluated by the experts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert high expert-rated performance for ChatGPT; the publication summarizes the same evaluation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bard also performed well, particularly in accuracy and clarity, while Bing Chat and Google SGE had lower overall scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bard performed well, especially in accuracy and clarity of the responses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms Bard's strong performance in the same dimensions mentioned in the preprint; differences about other models are not contradictory.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The presence of AI disclosure was less frequent in ChatGPT, which negatively affected its clarity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or highlight the AI-disclosure finding or its effect on clarity, so this specific claim appears to have been removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLM-supported tools potentially contribute to caregivers’ knowledge of pediatric oncology on related topics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLM-supported tools can enhance caregivers' knowledge of pediatric oncology. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the same conclusion but strengthens the wording and raises certainty from medium to high, amplifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research is needed to explore the application of these tools in other medical specialties and patient demographics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Each model has unique strengths and areas for improvement, indicating the need for careful selection based on specific clinical contexts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the general call for further research into a more specific recommendation about model selection and context-specific improvement, narrowing and clarifying the original limitation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Providing information on ML-based clinical decision support systems enhances trust in these systems among healthcare staff. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Providing staff with information about ML-based clinical decision support systems enhances their trust in these systems compared to an active control arm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific comparator (active control), making the original general claim more specific (clarified and narrowed) while keeping similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The intervention diminished distrust in ML-based clinical decision support systems compared to both the active control and blank control arms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The intervention diminished distrust in ML-based clinical decision support systems compared with the active control arm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication limits the claim to the active control (removing the explicit "both" comparison); the effect vs. blank control is reported separately in another publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study involved a randomized survey experiment with 780 participants from the Psychiatric Services of the Central Denmark Region. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim restates the methodological/sample-size detail; this operational detail is absent from the listed publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The intervention increased trust and decreased distrust in ML-based clinical decision support systems compared to the blank control arm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim about the blank control comparison is not directly mirrored in the preprint claim list (although related content was split across preprint statements).

alignments.pub_claim_text: Providing information on ML-based clinical decision support systems in hospital psychiatry may increase healthcare staff trust in such systems. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a more cautious, general concluding formulation ('may increase') that is not present verbatim in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine-learning models can detect sleep apnea and hypopnea events from overnight EEG and EMG recordings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning models can detect sleep apnea and hypopnea events from overnight EEG and EMG recordings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim with matching scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model 4 outperformed Model 3, which in turn outperformed Models 2 and 1 in detecting apnea/hypopnea events. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model 4 outperformed the other models in detecting apnea/hypopnea events, achieving a leave-one-patient-out cross-validated MCC of 0.314. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the model-ranking claim but adds a performance metric (clarification) without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Results indicate that sleep stage label alone is sufficient to detect apnea/hypopnea events. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Results indicate that sleep stage label alone is sufficient to detect apnea/hypopnea events. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical conclusion appearing in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EMG power was modulated by the beginning and end of apnea/hypopnea events, and similar modulation appeared in EEG power. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EMG power was modulated by the beginning and end of apnea/hypopnea events, and similar modulation appeared in EEG power. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result replicated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine-learning approaches to the detection of apnea/hypopnea events using overnight EEG must account for EMG contamination and sleep stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning approaches to the detection of apnea/hypopnea events using overnight EEG must account for EMG contamination and sleep stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion preserved between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our consensus classification model is able to distinguish cancer from control samples with 93% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our consensus classification model is able to distinguish cancer from control samples with 93% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact same performance claim in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An integrative approach using metabolomic profiles and ML-based classifiers has been employed to develop a clinical tool that assigns a probability that an individual patient does or does not have OC. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An integrative approach using metabolomic profiles and machine learning-based classifiers has been employed to develop a clinical tool for ovarian cancer diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the integrative-approach-to-OC-diagnostics claim but presents it with lower stated certainty (tempered confidence).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This personalized/probabilistic approach to cancer diagnostics is more clinically informative and accurate than traditional binary (yes/no) tests. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This personalized/probabilistic approach to cancer diagnostics is more clinically informative and accurate than traditional binary tests. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same comparative conclusion in both versions with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictive models derived from machine learning (ML) analyses of serum metabolic profiles can accurately detect ovarian cancer (OC). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A clinical tool has been developed that assigns a likelihood that an individual patient does or does not have cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames model-based detection as a developed clinical tool assigning likelihoods; this shifts wording from a general model-detection claim to the existence of a tool (clarification/ reframing).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The frequency distribution of model-derived patient scores can be used to develop a useful clinical tool for the diagnosis of OC. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims describe development of a tool but do not explicitly state the specific frequency-distribution-to-tool method; the explicit claim about using score distributions is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study proposes a novel Machine Learning (ML) assisted approach for analysing TCR-Seq data that can accurately distinguish between COVID-19-convalescent and healthy individuals. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study proposes a novel Machine Learning (ML)-assisted approach for analysing TCR-Seq data that can accurately distinguish between MIRA COVID-19-convalescent and healthy individuals based on differences in the triggered immune response. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds specificity about the assay (MIRA) and the biological basis (differences in triggered immune response), clarifying the original claim without changing its main thrust.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most SARS-CoV-2 antigens exhibit equal levels of recognition by MIRA TCRs in both convalescent and healthy cohorts, suggesting putative cross-reactivity between SARS-CoV-2 and other infectious agents. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some SARS-CoV-2 antigens were found to exhibit equal levels of recognition by MIRA TCRs in both convalescent and healthy cohorts, leading to the assumption of putative cross-reactivity between SARS-CoV-2 and other infectious agents. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication weakens the extent of the finding from 'most' to 'some', narrowing the scope and slightly tempering the claim about cross-reactivity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study provides evidence regarding the cross-reactivity between SARS-CoV-2 and a wide spectrum of pathogens and diseases, with M. tuberculosis and Influenza virus exhibiting the highest levels of cross-reactivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study provides evidence regarding putative cross-reactivity between SARS-CoV-2 and a wide spectrum of pathogens and diseases, with M. tuberculosis and Influenza virus exhibiting the highest levels of cross-reactivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Substantively identical result claim in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results can potentially shift the emphasis of immunological studies towards an increased application of TCR profiling assays. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These results can potentially shift the emphasis of immunological studies towards an increased application of TCR profiling assays that have the potential to uncover key mechanisms of cell-mediated immune response against pathogens and diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication extends the conclusion by adding a potential outcome (uncovering key mechanisms), broadening the implication while keeping similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates good prediction of past suicide attempts (SA) using multimodal data collected during pre/early-adolescence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data collected at a single timepoint during preadolescence can inform suicide attempt prediction during mid-late adolescence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction model achieved an AUC of 0.75 in the training dataset and 0.74 in the testing dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Suicide attempt prediction using subsets of selected features achieved good performance in the primary care sample with AUC = 0.75. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different machine learning algorithms identified various high-ranking features, indicating the importance of multiple data domains in predicting youth SA. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multiple data domains were represented among the highest-ranking features for suicide attempt prediction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings encourage the incorporation of neurocognitive and geocoded environmental measures in machine learning SA prediction pipelines. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific recommendation to incorporate neurocognitive and geocoded environmental measures is not present among the publication claims provided.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of highest-ranking features yielded similar prediction performance in external validation with AUC = 0.74 in the emergency department sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit external-validation result (AUC=0.74 in an emergency department sample) that is presented separately from the preprint claim mappings above.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Daily wearable sensor-outcomes robustly distinguished RA from healthy control participants with an F1 score of 0.807. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Daily wearable sensor-outcomes robustly distinguished RA from healthy control participants with an F1 score of 0.807. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The detection of RA severity levels could be improved by augmenting standard patient reported outcomes with sensor-based features, achieving an F1 score of 0.833 compared to 0.759 using PRO assessments alone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The detection of RA severity levels could be improved by augmenting standard patient reported outcomes with sensor-based features, achieving an F1 score of 0.833 compared to 0.759 using PRO assessments alone. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of modalities could reliably measure continuous RA severity, as determined by the clinician-assessed RAPID-3 score at baseline with an r2 of 0.692. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of modalities could reliably measure continuous RA severity, as determined by the clinician-assessed RAPID-3 score at baseline with an r2 of 0.692. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Digital measures of health status captured during daily life could greatly augment current in-clinic assessments for rheumatoid arthritis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Digital measures of health status captured during daily life could greatly augment current in-clinic assessments for rheumatoid arthritis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: MARKER-HF, SHFM, and MAGGIC exhibited similar discriminations with AUCs of 0.70, 0.71, and 0.71, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: All three scores showed good calibration across the full risk spectrum. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The all-cause death model showed the highest discrimination with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.895. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The all-cause death model showed the highest discrimination with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.903. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Xgboost ML model had the best predictive power for predicting CVD events three years into the future among the population with diabetes. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimization procedure found limited improvement in AUC by ethnicity. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is no trade-off between model predictive performance and equity gap of prediction by ethnicity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is no trade-off between model predictive performance and equity gap of prediction by ethnicity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The list of variables of importance was different among different models/ethnic groups. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: ML with administrative health data can be used for meaningful future prediction of health outcomes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Iron biomarkers were among the top predictors of peripheral arterial disease (PAD) in both the HEIST-DiC and NHANES cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Ferritin showed a positive interaction among females in both the HEIST-DiC and NHANES cohorts, indicating a potential sex-specific association with PAD. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain ferritin ranges in females were positively associated with PAD, demonstrating a nonlinear relationship between ferritin and PAD. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain ferritin ranges in females were positively associated with PAD, demonstrating a nonlinear relationship between ferritin and PAD. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest sex differences in PAD and highlight a possible role of iron in this relationship. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An increase in antibody response was found in symptomatic subjects positive for IgG at the beginning of the study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals positive for IgG at the beginning of the study show an increase in antibody response, particularly in those with loss of taste or smell.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals positive for IgG at the beginning of the study show an increase in antibody response, particularly in those with loss of taste or smell.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings may be linked to the lingering of SARS-CoV-2 in the olfactory bulb.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The increase in antibody response may be linked to the lingering of SARS-CoV-2 in the olfactory bulb.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial intelligence can detect left ventricular systolic dysfunction from electrocardiograms.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this core result with the same wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The noise-adapted model performed significantly better on test sets augmented with real-world noise recordings compared to the standard model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The noise-adapted model performs significantly better on test sets augmented with real-world noise recordings compared to the standard model.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The standard and noise-adapted models have an AUROC of 0.72 and 0.87, respectively, when evaluated on ECGs augmented with portable ECG device noise at an SNR of 0.5.
