alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach represents a novel strategy for the development of wearable adapted tools from clinical ECG repositories.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach represents a novel strategy for the development of wearable-adapted tools from clinical ECG repositories.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application and funding rates of health economics research were decreasing prior to the release of NIH guidance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application and funding rates of health economics research were decreasing prior to the release of NIH guidance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Following the publication of NIH guidance, the application and funding rate of health economics applications increased.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Following the publication of NIH guidance, the application and funding rate of health economics applications increased.

alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 211 highly interrelated genes implicated in opioid addiction that are associated with the Akt, BDNF, and ERK pathways.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our synthesis of multiomics using a systems biology approach revealed key gene targets that could contribute to drug repurposing and genetics-informed addiction treatment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MIDAS and KNN performed the best as the rate of random missingness increased and when blockwise missingness was simulated.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MIDAS and KNN performed the best as the random missingness rate increased and when blockwise missingness was simulated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MIDAS and KNN both provide promising imputation performance in mental and behavioral health survey data that exhibit blockwise missingness patterns.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MIDAS and KNN both provide promising imputation performance in mental and behavioral health survey data that exhibit blockwise missingness patterns.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about promising imputation performance is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant group-level differences were demonstrated in self-report, N-back and verbal fluency behavior, and task-related brain activation between MCI patients and healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifier performance was strongest when neural metrics were included, achieving an AUC of 0.92. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential of TD-fNIRS to assess MCI with short brain scans in clinical settings. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost classifier produced superior internal performance with AUC scores ranging from 84% to 97% compared to other ML models reported in the literature. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The external performance of the model was 86% when trained and tested on fourteen hematological parameters from the same country (Brazil). (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External performances of the model were reduced to 69% when trained on datasets from Italy and tested on datasets from Brazil, and to 65% when trained on datasets from Italy and tested on datasets from Western Europe. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The external performance of the model was reduced when trained on datasets from Italy and tested on Brazil and Western Europe, with AUC scores of 69% and 65 respectively. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study demonstrates that models trained and tested on the same population yield reasonably accurate results, indicating potential utility for COVID-19 detection. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single-site clinical models performed well within their own regions, but worse in general when evaluated on other regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single-site clinical models performed well within their own regions, but generally worse when evaluated in other regions. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained on different regions can have different optimal parameters and thresholds for performance in practice. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.rationale: The publication's language conveys the same finding about variation in model parameters and thresholds across sites.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a trade-off between internal and external performance, where a model with better external performance usually has worse internal performance but is generally more robust overall. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinical models for epilepsy diagnosis in LMICs demonstrate limited generalisability and a trade-off between internal and external performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relationship between predictors and model outcomes varies across sites, suggesting the need to update specific aspects of the model with local data before broader implementation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relationship between predictors and model outcomes varies across sites, suggesting the need to update specific model aspects with local data before broader implementation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis identifies failure modes of the metrics, highlighting the types of information that are not captured. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis of failure modes of the metrics provides guidance for metric selection and interpretation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both describe analysis of failure modes and their practical implications for metric interpretation and selection.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work is expected to guide the evaluation and development of report generation systems that can generate reports from medical images approaching the level of radiologists. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on how the proposed metrics address limitations in evaluation (a more specific framing) rather than the broader forward-looking statement about guiding development of human-level report generators.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Topological data analysis (TDA) combined with machine learning accurately predicts the risk of relapse in children and adolescents with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL), particularly for patients previously classified as 'low risk'. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive power of CD10, CD20, CD38, and CD45 as biomarkers for ALL diagnosis is independently confirmed. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.rationale: Both claims match directly in content and certainty, confirming those biomarkers' predictive power.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three increasingly detailed prognostic pipelines for analyzing flow cytometry data from ALL patients are proposed, depending on technical and technological availability. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TDA enables high-accuracy predictions of relapse to be made at the time of diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LGB model produced higher, but comparable performance compared to the Epic model in predicting 30-day readmission risk. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest model, using features from the first 48 hours, produced a higher AUROC (0.684) than the Epic model (AUROC: 0.676). (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Patient Optimizer (POP) algorithms effectively predicted any post-operative complication, kidney failure, and length of stay in the sample population. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: A larger study is justified to improve the algorithm to better predict complications and length of hospital stay. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: A larger dataset may also improve the prediction of additional specific complications, readmission, and mortality. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal ML-PS improved the ML-score by 17 ± 14% over the ref-PS. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.rationale: The published claim changes the comparator (KDIGO highest-risk strata rather than the clinical model) and adjusts the PPV values slightly, so the result is quantitatively and contextually revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning classifiers based on the plasma lipidomes of patients suffering from CVD and SLE allowed clear distinction of these two chronic inflammatory diseases from each other and from healthy age-matched controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of the lipidomes enabled separation of the studied chronic inflammatory diseases from controls based on independent validation test set classification performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the key result about separation using lipidome-based classifiers with independent validation, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrate convincing evidence for the capability of lipidomics to separate the studied chronic and inflammatory diseases from controls based on independent validation test set classification performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of the lipidomes enabled separation of the studied chronic inflammatory diseases from controls based on independent validation test set classification performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This is the same claim emphasizing independent validation and classification performance, preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dysregulation of the plasma lipidome is characteristic of chronic inflammatory diseases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dysregulation of the plasma lipidome is characteristic of chronic inflammatory diseases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this high-level conclusion about lipidome dysregulation unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lipid profiling accurately identifies the diseases and in the case of CVD also identifies sub-classes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lipid profiling accurately identifies the diseases and in the case of CVD also identifies sub-classes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the claim that lipid profiling distinguishes diseases and CVD sub-classes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dysregulated lipids are partially but not fully counterbalanced by statin treatment. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dysregulated lipids are partially but not fully counterbalanced by statin treatment. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication frames it as a limitation, which reduces the strength/interpretive confidence of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant associations were found between diabetes status in middle-aged adults and symptoms such as polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, sudden weight loss, partial paresis, polyphagia, and visual blurring. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Symptoms like polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, sudden weight loss, partial paresis, polyphagia, and visual blurring were significantly associated with diabetes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same symptom–diabetes associations with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All features studied, including demographics and symptoms, were confirmed as relevant for predicting diabetes in middle-aged adults. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diabetes associated symptoms provide valuable diagnostic opportunities for early diabetes detection in middle-aged adults. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert that all features (including demographics) were confirmed relevant; it narrows focus to symptom-based diagnostic value, weakening and narrowing the original broader claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Random Forest model showed the highest sensitivity (98.59%) and demonstrated the best accuracy (96.58%) and area under the curve score (96.00%), making it the most efficient ML model for predicting diabetes among middle-aged adults. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest model exhibited the highest sensitivity (98.59%) and outperformed other models in accuracy (96.58%) and area under the curve score (96.00%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same model performance metrics and conclusion about model superiority.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings of this study emphasize the importance of using diabetes-related symptoms for early detection of diabetes within the middle-aged adult population. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diabetes associated symptoms provide valuable diagnostic opportunities for early diabetes detection in middle-aged adults. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion but presents it with stronger language/certainty, increasing confidence in the diagnostic value of symptoms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We discovered four neuroanatomical subtypes of primary progressive aphasia, labelled S1, S2, S3, S4, exhibiting robustness to statistical scrutiny. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We discovered four neuroanatomical subtypes of primary progressive aphasia, labelled S1, S2, S3, and S4, exhibiting robustness to statistical scrutiny. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same discovery and characterization of four subtypes with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: S1 correlated strongly with semantic variant, while S2, S3, and S4 showed mixed associations with the logopenic and non-fluent/agrammatic variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: S1 was correlated strongly with the semantic variant, whereas S2, S3, and S4 showed mixed associations with the logopenic and non-fluent/agrammatic variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same subtype–variant associations with matching wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At first follow up, 84% of patients' subtype assignment was stable, and 91.9% of patients' stage assignment was stable. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At first follow-up, subtype assignment was stable for 84% of patients, and stage assignment was stable for 91.9% of patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the stability metrics and wording unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identification of multiple profiles within the logopenic and non-fluent/agrammatic variants supports recent conceptualisations of primary progressive aphasia as a spectrum of disorders rather than discrete entities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings suggest that separable spatiotemporal neuroanatomical phenotypes do exist within the primary progressive aphasia spectrum, but that these are noisy, particularly for the non-fluent/agrammatic and logopenic variants. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the spectrum interpretation but adds nuance about noise/uncertainty in some variants, which tempers the strength of the original conceptual claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Understanding the multifaceted profiles of the disease holds potential implications for clinical decision support. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Understanding the multifaceted profiles of the disease has potential implications for clinical decision support. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same translational implication with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PREDICT algorithm achieved 63% accuracy and 89% negative predictive value for predicting treatment interruption in ART clients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claim set does not include the specific PREDICT algorithm performance metrics present in the preprint, indicating the claim was omitted from the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The threshold approach consistently and accurately assigned levels of interruption in treatment risk for multiple stages of the care cascade. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The threshold approach consistently and accurately assigned levels of interruption in treatment (IIT) risk for multiple stages of the care cascade. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Texts are effectively identical in content and certainty, indicating the claim was carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The archetype approach identified subgroups at increased risk of treatment interruption, particularly those late to previous appointments or returning after disengagement. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The archetype approach identified several subgroups at increased risk of IIT, including those late to previous appointments and those returning after a period of disengagement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same subgroup-identification finding with very similar wording; the core result and scope remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Behavioural elements of the archetypes were more consistent predictors of treatment interruption risk than demographic factors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Behavioural elements of the archetypes tended to drive the risk of treatment interruption more consistently than demographics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions convey the same conclusion that behavioral factors more consistently predict risk than demographics, with comparable certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining routinely collected medical record data with demographic and socioeconomic data can assess individual risk of future treatment disengagement. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Routinely collected medical record data can be combined with basic demographic and socioeconomic data to assess individual risk of future treatment disengagement. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion about using routine medical and demographic/socioeconomic data to assess individual disengagement risk.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The transmission coefficient in SEIR-like models evolves with time, presenting random patterns and stylized facts such as mean-reversion and jumps. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The transmission coefficient in SEIR-like models evolves with time and presents random patterns. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result that the transmission coefficient evolves stochastically but omits some of the preprint's listed 'stylized facts' (e.g., explicit mention of mean-reversion and jumps), so the claim is slightly clarified/condensed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed jump-diffusion stochastic processes effectively parameterize the transmission coefficient in an SEIR-like model that accounts for death and time-dependent parameters. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed jump-diffusion stochastic processes can effectively parameterize the transmission coefficient in an SEIR-like model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same methodological contribution (jump-diffusion parameterization) with comparable certainty; the publication is slightly more concise but not substantively different.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed jump-diffusion model presented remarkably accurate out-of-sample predictions, even during larger forecasted periods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The forecasted scenarios using the proposed model were fairly accurate despite the simplicity of the epidemiological model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves that forecasts were accurate but tones down 'remarkably' to 'fairly' and notes model simplicity; this is a clarified/tempered restatement of the preprint forecasting claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model demonstrates the existence and uniqueness of solutions and studies its asymptotic behavior. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a mathematical result (existence/uniqueness and asymptotics) not stated in the preprint claims; this is an added formal analysis claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study achieved 81% accuracy in classifying patients with tinnitus versus malingerers using only 10 minutes of data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study achieved 81% accuracy in classifying patients with tinnitus and malingerers using only 10 minutes of data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identical performance result with the same numeric accuracy and data duration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clear differences were identified between participants with tinnitus and those feigning tinnitus in both overt ratings and covert fingertip movement trajectories. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clear differences were identified between participants with tinnitus and those feigning tinnitus in both overt reporting and covert fingertip movement trajectories. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about observed differences in overt/covert measures is expressed in effectively the same terms in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Objective measurements of tinnitus will improve estimates of tinnitus prevalence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Quantitative, automated measurements of tinnitus salience could improve clinical outcome assays. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts the target from population-level prevalence estimation to improving clinical outcome assays, changing the claim's aim (hence revised and narrowed) though certainty remains similar.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of tablet-based self-directed hearing assessments and neural network classifiers can effectively differentiate between actual tinnitus patients and those feigning symptoms. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of tablet-based self-directed hearing assessments and neural network classifiers can effectively differentiate between actual tinnitus patients and malingerers. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same methodological contribution but lowers the expressed certainty (high→medium); the core claim is preserved but tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4.0 produced longer responses than Gemini Pro and Claude 2, averaging 577 ± 81.19 words. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claim set does not report model output length statistics, so this specific preprint result was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4.0 achieved significantly higher mean composite scores compared to Gemini Pro and Claude 2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4.0 achieved significantly higher composite scores compared to Gemini Pro and Claude 2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same statistically significant superiority in composite scores, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Specialists consistently awarded higher ratings than resident trainees across all assessed domains and overall composite scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not include a specific comparison between specialists and residents, so this rater-group finding appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clinical experience and domain expertise of individual clinicians significantly shaped the interpretation of AI-generated responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinical experience and domain expertise of individual clinicians significantly shaped the interpretation of AI-generated responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is verbatim in the publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are disconcerting human vulnerabilities in safeguarding against potentially harmful outputs from AI systems. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the current stage, none of the tested AI models should be considered safe for direct clinical deployment in the absence of human supervision. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication converts a general caution about human vulnerabilities into a stronger, prescriptive conclusion that models are unsafe for direct clinical deployment, broadening and amplifying the original concern.

alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4.0 outperformed Gemini Pro and Claude 2 in factual consistency, comprehensiveness, and the absence of medical harm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a more detailed breakdown of performance across subdomains (factual consistency, comprehensiveness, absence of harm) that was not explicitly stated as separate claims in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of posts (97% ARBs, 91% ACEIs) indicated experiencing an adverse event as the reason for medication change. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The majority of posts (97% ARBs, 91% ACEIs) indicated experiencing an adverse event as the reason for medication change. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same numeric finding about proportion of posts citing adverse events as reason for medication change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Musculoskeletal and respiratory symptoms were the most commonly reported adverse effects in social media postings associated with drug discontinuation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most common adverse events reported for ARBs were musculoskeletal and connective tissue disorders, while for ACEIs, they were respiratory, thoracic, and mediastinal disorders. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same core finding but clarifies it by distinguishing the most common adverse-event categories by drug class (ARBs vs ACEIs).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study shows the promise of WebMD mining as an effective tool in medication nonadherence and adverse event research. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate a general claim about the promise of WebMD mining as presented in the preprint; instead it emphasizes different conclusions and comparisons, so the preprint's general endorsement appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: These adverse event categories appeared more frequently on WebMD data than FDA data, with a statistically significant difference (p < 0.001). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a comparative statistical result (WebMD vs FDA frequencies) that was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Managing musculoskeletal and respiratory symptoms is a potential target of interventions seeking to improve medication persistence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication draws a policy/clinical implication about intervention targets that is not explicitly asserted in the preprint claim set, so this is an added conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fine-tuned Segment Anything Model (SAM) was superior or comparable to MedSAM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The fine-tuned Segment Anything Model (SAM) was superior or comparable to MedSAM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sentence is effectively identical between versions with no change in meaning, scope, or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fine-tuned SAM outperformed EchoNet and U-Net models, demonstrating strong generalization, especially on apical 2-chamber (A2C) images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The fine-tuned SAM outperformed EchoNet and U-Net models, demonstrating strong generalization, especially on apical 2-chamber (A2C) images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim text and emphasis are the same in preprint and publication, so meaning and confidence are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SAM-enhanced workflow reduced annotation time by 50% while maintaining segmentation quality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SAM-enhanced workflow reduced annotation time by 50% while maintaining segmentation quality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is verbatim between versions; no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An effective strategy for fine-tuning a vision foundation model for enhancing clinical workflow efficiency and supporting human-AI collaboration was demonstrated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated an effective strategy for fine-tuning a vision foundation model for enhancing clinical workflow efficiency and supporting human-AI collaboration. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim content is the same but the publication lowers the expressed certainty (high -> medium), so the conclusion is retained but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are no automated metrics to evaluate the usefulness of explanations in the clinical context. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are no automated metrics to evaluate the usefulness of explanations in the clinical context. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Identical limitation statement in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our new scoring method can evaluate the usefulness of explanations generated by any XAI method that provides importance values for the input features of the prediction model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our new scoring method can evaluate the usefulness of explanations generated by any XAI method that provides importance values for the input features of the prediction model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The scores obtained using our method corroborate with independent evidence from clinical literature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The scores obtained using our method corroborate with independent evidence from clinical literature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result statement is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method can be used to evaluate and select useful explanations from a diverse set of XAI techniques in clinical contexts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method can be used to evaluate and select useful explanations from a diverse set of XAI techniques in clinical contexts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion unchanged between preprint and published paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EHR-ML facilitates predictive modelling by leveraging institutional Electronic Health Record (EHR) data to generate clinical insights. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The effectiveness of the EHR-ML is demonstrated through a series of case studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes facilitation as demonstrated effectiveness via case studies, making the claim more concrete and focused on demonstrated examples rather than a broad facilitation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Benchmarking analyses demonstrate EHR-ML’s efficacy in predicting outcomes like inpatient mortality and the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) Length of Stay (LOS). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EHR-ML consistently surpasses the performance of traditional methodologies in developing high-performance models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes consistent superiority over traditional methods (a broader performance claim) relative to the preprint's specific benchmarking on certain outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models built with EHR-ML outperformed conventional methods, showcasing its generalisability and versatility even in challenging scenarios such as high class-imbalance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EHR-ML exhibits strong generalisability across diverse healthcare settings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both emphasize generalisability and outperforming conventional methods; the publication emphasizes 'across diverse settings' rather than listing specific challenging scenarios, a clarification rather than contradiction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EHR-ML is a critical step towards democratising predictive modelling in healthcare, enabling rapid hypothesis testing and facilitating the generation of biomedical knowledge. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EHR-ML facilitates rapid hypothesis testing aimed to generate localised biomedical knowledge. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the broad 'democratising predictive modelling' claim to the specific benefit of enabling rapid, localised hypothesis testing and knowledge generation.

alignments.pub_claim_text: EHR-ML enhances the clinical relevance and accuracy of predictive models by incorporating local context into machine learning applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion about incorporating local context does not have an explicit counterpart in the preprint and appears as an added emphasis in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The EuCanImage consortium is developing AI algorithms for precision medicine in oncology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The EuCanImage consortium is developing AI algorithms for precision medicine in oncology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Identical contribution described in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The initiative addresses seven unmet clinical needs across three different cancer types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The initiative addresses 7 unmet clinical needs across 3 different cancer types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same factual statement (numeric counts) in both versions; minor change in phrasing only.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An innovative process has been implemented to capture clinical data and transform it into the EuCanImage data models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An innovative process has been implemented to capture and standardize clinical data from hospitals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication rephrases 'transform into data models' as 'standardize clinical data', clarifying the nature of the process without changing the result's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow integrates recognized software, data standards, and custom tools for data transformation and quality control. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of well-defined clinical data standards is essential for achieving interoperability in health care data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts from a specific description of the workflow to a broader conclusion about the importance of standards for interoperability, changing emphasis and lowering certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant methodological challenges and ethico-legal constraints impede the real-world implementation of data models. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This limitation described in the preprint does not appear in the publication's claim list and thus is treated as removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The single-image super-resolution reconstruction framework enhances the through-plane resolution of structural MRI in people with multiple sclerosis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The single-image super-resolution reconstruction framework enhances the through-plane resolution of structural MRI in people with multiple sclerosis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The super-resolution strategy leads to more accurate MRI reconstructions than competing methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The super-resolution strategy leads to more accurate MRI reconstructions than competing methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result unchanged in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework improves lesion segmentation on low-resolution MRI, approaching the performance achievable with high-resolution images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework improves lesion segmentation on low-resolution MRI, approaching the performance achievable with high-resolution images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SR framework facilitates the use of low-resolution retrospective MRI to investigate quantitative image-based biomarkers of multiple sclerosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate the potential of the super-resolution framework to facilitate the use of low-resolution retrospective MRI to investigate quantitative image-based biomarkers of multiple sclerosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication softens the claim to 'demonstrate the potential', reducing strength of the assertion compared with the preprint's more definite phrasing, hence clarified and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A proteomic risk score based on 20 blood proteomic biomarkers predicts the progression to severe COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim reports that the 20-protein score predicts progression to severe COVID-19; the publication instead emphasizes associations with cytokines and gut microbiota, so the original prediction claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proteomic risk score is positively associated with proinflammatory cytokines mainly among older individuals.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proteomic risk score based on 20 blood proteomic biomarkers is positively associated with proinflammatory cytokines among older individuals.
alignments.rationale: The publication claim restates the same association, population (older individuals), and biomarker set with no substantive change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A core set of gut microbiota can accurately predict the proteomic biomarkers associated with severe COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A core set of gut microbiota can accurately predict proteomic biomarkers using a machine learning model.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core claim that gut taxa predict proteomic biomarkers but explicitly notes prediction via a machine-learning model; the severe-COVID context is implicit in the targeted biomarkers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gut microbiota features are highly correlated with proinflammatory cytokines.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gut microbiota features are highly correlated with proinflammatory cytokines in an independent set of individuals.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same correlation claim but clarifies that it was observed/replicated in an independent cohort, strengthening evidence without changing the claim's substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gut microbiota may underlie the predisposition of normal individuals to severe COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gut microbiota composition and function are closely related to inflammation and molecular signatures of host response to infection among healthy individuals.
alignments.rationale: The publication tones down the causal/predisposition claim to an association with inflammation and host-response signatures among healthy individuals, shifting from a direct predisposition to a more circumscribed mechanistic/associative statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning-based model can satisfactorily predict SCD progression using combined neuroimage features and clinical measurements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SCD progression was satisfactorily predicted with the combined features of neuroimaging and clinical measurements.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result: combined imaging and clinical features yielded satisfactory prediction of SCD progression, with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Factors such as a history of stroke, low education level, low baseline MoCA score, shrunk left amygdala, and enlarged white matter at the banks of the right superior temporal sulcus are associated with the progression of SCD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A history of stroke, low education level, low baseline MoCA score, shrunk left amygdala, and enlarged white matter at the banks of the right superior temporal sulcus were found to favor the progression from SCD to MCI.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same risk factors and frames them as favoring progression, matching the preprint's conclusion in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study represents the largest retrospective analysis of SCD-to-MCI conversion with the longest follow-up to date.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study is the largest retrospective study of SCD-to-MCI conversion with the longest follow-up to date.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution claim verbatim; there is no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that predictable far-future cognitive decline can be identified in high-risk populations using baseline measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest predictable far-future cognitive decline for risky populations based on baseline measures only.
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the same tentative conclusion about identifying far-future decline from baseline measures in high-risk groups, with equivalent caution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mathematical model reveals two transmissibility parameters influenced by management strategies for controlling the COVID-19 pandemic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A mathematical model describing viral infection dynamics reveals two transmissibility parameters influenced by management strategies.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the model result about two transmissibility parameters influenced by management strategies with essentially identical meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model parameters are correlated to different management strategies, enabling comparison and suggesting timely alterations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning employed with a mechanistic framework of a mathematical model enhances understanding of complex systems.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a higher-level methodological claim about combining ML and mechanistic models to enhance understanding; this overlaps with the preprint's interpretive claim but is broader and less specific about suggesting actionable alterations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Restricted non-essential business closure, school closing, and strictures on mass gathering influence the spread of infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model indicates that restricted non-essential business closure, school closing, and strictures on mass gathering influence the spread of infection.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about the influence of those specific interventions on infection spread.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can speculate on the timing of an economic reboot based on the status of the infection.
