alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the causal MR finding; the specific method CAUSE is not named but the substantive causal conclusion is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most significant contributing variants near ELN, FBN1, and ULK4 are implicated in connective tissue and blood pressure pathways.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study implicates localizing genes near ELN, PRDM6, and ADAMTS7 in connective tissue and blood pressure pathways.
alignments.rationale: The set of implicated genes differs substantially between preprint and publication (FBN1/ULK4 vs PRDM6/ADAMTS7), representing a substantive revision of the gene-level findings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepFlow cardiac flow phenotyping at scale reinforces the contribution of connective tissue genes, blood pressure, and root size to aortic valve function in the general population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepFlow cardiac flow phenotyping at scale reinforces the contribution of connective tissue genes, blood pressure, and root size to aortic valve function.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the overall conclusion that phenotyping supports contributions of connective tissue genes, blood pressure, and root size to valve function.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system achieved a sensitivity of 0.974 and specificity of 0.922 on the test dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI system achieves a sensitivity of 0.974 and specificity of 0.922 on the test dataset.
alignments.rationale: The published paper repeats the exact performance metrics reported in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system automatically highlights all lesion regions for faster examination.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI system can save about 30%-40% of the detection time for physicians.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the practical outcome (time savings) likely resulting from features such as lesion highlighting; this reframes the implementation detail into an operational benefit, broadening the focus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system has been deployed in 16 hospitals and is performing over 1,300 screenings per day.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific deployment scale (16 hospitals, 1,300/day), so this operational-scale claim was not included.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interdisciplinary team was able to deploy the AI system in four weeks despite the challenges of the epidemic crisis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The interdisciplinary team was able to deploy the AI system in four weeks despite various challenges.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the deployment-time claim and the notion of overcoming challenges, matching the preprint's assertion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI system provides the probability of infection for each CT image, allowing physicians to confirm and segregate infected patients in time.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific operational feature (per-image infection probability and patient segregation utility) that was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 overran the Ecuadorian public health system, making it one of the most affected in Latin America.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not repeat or support the broad claim that Ecuador was one of the most affected countries; no corresponding statement is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Public health control measures, including social distancing and lockdowns, were implemented at different times in Guayaquil and Quito.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or discuss temporal differences in measures between Guayaquil and Quito, so the preprint claim is not present in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Hospital General del Sur de Quito transitioned from a general to a specific COVID-19 health center to meet the health demand from patients with respiratory afflictions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Hospital General del Sur de Quito transitioned to a COVID-19 exclusive hospital to meet the health demand from patients with respiratory afflictions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same transformation of the hospital with equivalent scope and intent; wording is slightly different but the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A triage strategy based on a severity score calculated through AI-assisted chest CT was implemented at the HGSQ.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the AI-assisted chest CT were 21.4% and 66.7%, respectively, when considering a score >70%.
alignments.rationale: The publication moves from reporting an implemented AI-assisted CT triage strategy to presenting its measured diagnostic performance, which refines and narrows the original implementation claim and indicates limited accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The clinical, epidemiological, and laboratory data presented are from 75 laboratory tested COVID-19 patients, representing the first outbreak of Quito city.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differences in laboratory parameters were found between intensive care unit (ICU) and non-ICU COVID-19 cases, specifically in C-reactive protein, lactate dehydrogenase, and lymphocytes.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports results derived from the clinical dataset (laboratory parameter differences) but does not restate the sample-size/first-outbreak framing; the dataset claim is thus subsumed into reported results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 9 significant, independent loci near genes that are associated with maintaining sarcomeric function under biomechanical stress.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified nine significant, independent loci near genes that are associated with maintaining sarcomeric function under biomechanical stress.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same genetic loci finding with equivalent meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Age, sex and diabetes were independent predictors of diastolic function.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Age, sex and diabetes were independent predictors of diastolic function.
alignments.rationale: The predictor relationships are identically reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found a causal relationship between ventricular stiffness and heart failure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found a causal relationship between genetically determined ventricular stiffness and incident heart failure.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the causal relationship pertains to genetically determined ventricular stiffness and incident (new) heart failure, narrowing and clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results provide novel insights into the genetic and environmental factors influencing diastolic function.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results provide insights into the genetic and environmental factors influencing diastolic function.
alignments.rationale: The publication tones down the adjective 'novel' but conveys the same overall conclusion about insights into genetic and environmental influences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study constructed a knowledge network comprised of 3,723 genes and 99 diseases using a cognitive computing text-mining application.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A cognitive computing text-mining application was used to construct a knowledge network comprising 3,723 genes and 99 diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact methodological contribution and network size; the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach helped to unravel the molecular bases of diseases over time and detect shared mechanisms between clinically distinct diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The systems approach helped to unravel the molecular bases of diseases and detect shared mechanisms between clinically distinct diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about the systems approach revealing molecular bases and shared mechanisms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multi-purpose therapeutic drugs target genes which are commonly associated with several psychiatric, inflammatory, or infectious disorders.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multi-purpose therapeutic drugs target genes that are commonly associated with several psychiatric, inflammatory, or infectious disorders.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about multi-purpose therapeutic drug targets with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study tracked yearly changes in the knowledge network to analyze how knowledge of gene-disease associations has evolved over the past 30 years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study tracked yearly changes in the knowledge network to analyze how understanding of gene-disease associations has evolved over the past 30 years.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the temporal tracking of the knowledge network and its role in analyzing evolution of gene-disease associations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Path integration changes may precede a clinical presentation of Alzheimer’s disease by several years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Path integration changes may precede a clinical presentation of Alzheimer's disease by several years.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same tentative conclusion about path integration preceding clinical AD.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vestibular input is critical for intact path integration.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vestibular input is critical for intact path integration.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion regarding vestibular input and path integration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The vestibular features derived from sensor data accurately classified Apolipoprotein E ε3ε4 carriers and ε3ε3 carrier controls with 65% to 79% accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models illustrate that the vestibular features accurately classified Apolipoprotein E ε3ε4 carriers and ε3ε3 carrier controls with 65% to 79% accuracy depending on task trial.
alignments.rationale: The reported classification accuracies and role of vestibular features are preserved in the publication, with added detail about task dependence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results demonstrate the cross-sectional role of the vestibular system in Alzheimer’s disease risk carriers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future investigations should examine if vestibular functions explain individual phenotypic heterogeneity in path integration among Alzheimer's disease risk carriers.
alignments.rationale: The preprint framed the results as demonstrating a role; the publication reframes this more cautiously as a topic warranting future investigation, thus tempering the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings may explain individual phenotypic heterogeneity in path integration within the Alzheimer’s disease risk population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future investigations should examine if vestibular functions explain individual phenotypic heterogeneity in path integration among Alzheimer's disease risk carriers.
alignments.rationale: The publication converts the tentative explanatory suggestion into a recommendation for future study, clarifying and slightly tempering the original assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher values of Global Health Security Index (GHSI) were expected to translate to lower COVID-19 severity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher values of Global Health Security Index (GHSI) were expected to translate to lower COVID-19 severity.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same expectation about GHSI and COVID-19 severity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher estimated country preparedness to epidemics may lead to higher disease mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher estimated country preparedness to epidemics may lead to higher disease mortality.
alignments.rationale: The result about the counterintuitive relationship between preparedness scores and mortality is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The observed puzzle is largely an artifact of oversimplified data analysis and misclassified COVID-19 deaths.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The observed relationship between GHSI scores and COVID-19 severity is largely an artifact of oversimplified data analysis and misclassified COVID-19 deaths.
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the same explanation for the observed GHSI–severity puzzle; meaning and scope remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Countries with high GHSI scores have a higher median age of the population and earlier epidemics onset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Countries with high GHSI scores have a higher median age of the population and earlier epidemics onset.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this descriptive demographic/temporal association unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis revealed no significant differences in immunophenotypic patterns between relapse and non-relapse patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis revealed no significant differences in immunophenotypic patterns between relapse and non-relapse patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is verbatim the same result as the preprint, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study suggests the need for alternative approaches to handle flow cytometry data in relapse prediction. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study suggests the need for alternative approaches to handle flow cytometry data in relapse prediction. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same suggestion/concusion with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This paper explores the potential prognostic value of flow cytometry data at diagnosis for relapse prediction in B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This paper explores the potential prognostic value of flow cytometry data at diagnosis for relapse prediction in B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is the same contribution statement as the preprint without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model achieved an accuracy of 88.0% on the glioma grading task using the BraTS dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model was trained on the BraTS dataset with 88.0% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the model accuracy on the same dataset with the same numeric value and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Physicians' task performance increased from an average accuracy of 82.5% to 87.7% when assisted by AI prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Physicians' task performance increased to 87.7±7.3% with statistical significance (p-value = 0.002) when assisted by AI prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds statistical detail (±, p-value) supporting significance, clarifying and strengthening the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The addition of AI explanation assistance did not yield a significant performance boost, with physicians' accuracy remaining at 88.5%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The additional assistance of AI explanation did not significantly change physicians' performance, remaining at 88.5±7.0% (p-value = 0.35). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides uncertainty statistics (± and p-value) but the substantive conclusion of no significant change remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the clinical utility of AI in assisting physicians with glioma grading tasks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The observed improvement in physicians' task performance was mainly because physicians' decision patterns converged to be similar to AI. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the high-level claim of clinical utility into a more specific mechanistic explanation and presents it with lower certainty, narrowing and tempering the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evaluation reveals limitations of applying existing AI explanation techniques in clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The evaluation identified the limitations and clinical usage gaps of existing explainable AI techniques for future improvement. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the limitation finding with slightly expanded wording about usage gaps and future improvement, clarifying but not changing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trustworthiness of AI algorithms in clinical settings is linked to their explainability and interpretability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trustworthiness of AI algorithms in clinical settings is linked to their explainability and interpretability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level conclusion about explainability and trustworthiness with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most validation studies for medical imaging AI focus on performance metrics rather than the accuracy of explanations provided by the algorithms. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most validation studies for medical imaging AI focus on performance metrics rather than the accuracy of explanations provided by the algorithms. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same limitation claim about validation studies without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Explainability Failure Ratio (EFR) is a new metric proposed to evaluate the explainability of AI algorithms in medical imaging. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Explainability Failure Ratio (EFR) is a new metric proposed to evaluate the explainability of AI algorithms in clinical settings. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication slightly broadens the application wording from 'medical imaging' to 'clinical settings', clarifying the intended application without changing the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A lower EFR was observed for the model with lower sensitivity in detecting consolidation on chest X-rays, suggesting that trustworthiness should consider both statistical metrics and clinically-oriented models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A lower EFR was observed for the model with lower sensitivity in detecting consolidation on chest X-rays, indicating that trustworthiness should consider both statistical metrics and clinically-oriented models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the observed result and its implication with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Socioeconomic status (SES) plays a major role in AI algorithm bias and the (in)completeness of electronic health records (EHRs) data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Asthmatic children with lower SES had larger balanced error rates than those with higher SES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a specific empirical result (balanced error rates by SES) that clarifies and narrows the preprint's broader claim about SES driving bias and EHR incompleteness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Understanding the impact of SES on algorithmic bias is important for assessing and mitigating such bias. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lower SES is associated with worse predictive model performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the empirical association that underpins the preprint's conclusion about the importance of understanding SES impact, making the point more specific and narrower.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The role of SES in AI fairness science literature is currently under-recognized and under-studied. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly states that the role of SES is under-recognized in the literature, so this preprint claim is not represented in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The HOUSES index is a validated individual-level socioeconomic measure that allows AI researchers to assess algorithmic bias due to SES. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The HOUSES index allows AI researchers to assess bias in predictive model performance by SES. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution claim about the HOUSES index enabling assessment of SES-related bias with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study results highlight a novel conceptual strategy for quantifying AI bias by SES. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study results highlight a potential strategy for identifying bias using an innovative SES measure. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the claim slightly ("potential strategy" and "innovative SES measure") but retains the same core contribution regarding a new approach to identify bias.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The literature on Yoga is complex and lacks general guidelines for researchers due to the diversity of methods and areas of interest. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a lack of research exploring the mechanisms behind the learning of yoga practitioners and its potential for further enhancement. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication identifies a specific gap (mechanisms of learning) that narrows and clarifies the preprint's broader observation about complexity and lack of general guidelines.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Contemporary methods of Artificial Intelligence, including text mining, have advanced the organization of scientific literature on Yoga. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vector text mining has made it possible to bolster and improve human analysis in the context of yoga research. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same result about text-mining/vector methods improving literature organization/analysis with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides platforms for Yoga researchers to evaluate findings and explore the literature more effectively. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to develop a comprehensive framework for existing literature on yoga to facilitate multidisciplinary collaboration. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution as a comprehensive framework to facilitate collaboration, which clarifies and slightly broadens the preprint's statement about providing platforms for exploration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research reveals insights into broader aspects of the relationships within the Yoga literature. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis of literature revealed 4 dimensions (exercise, physiology, theory, and therapeutic) divided into 9 cohesive groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the high-level 'insights' by enumerating dimensions and groups and reports this as a clear result, making the preprint's general statement more specific and stronger.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Raman profiles of blood cells can distinguish between healthy individuals, disease controls, and ME/CFS patients with high accuracy (91%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Raman profiles of blood cells can distinguish between healthy individuals, disease controls, and ME/CFS patients with high accuracy (91%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication text are effectively identical in result, accuracy, and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Raman profiles can further differentiate between mild, moderate, and severe ME/CFS patients with an accuracy of 84%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Raman profiles can further differentiate between mild, moderate, and severe ME/CFS patients with an accuracy of 84%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about stratification accuracy is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Specific Raman peaks correlate with ME/CFS phenotypes and have the potential to provide insights into biological changes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Specific Raman peaks that correlate with ME/CFS phenotypes have the potential to provide insights into biological changes and support the development of new therapeutics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the original point about biological insight but explicitly extends the potential application to therapeutic development, broadening the implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study presents a promising approach for aiding in the diagnosis and management of ME/CFS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study presents a promising approach for aiding in the diagnosis and management of ME/CFS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about promise for diagnosis and management is unchanged in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach could be extended to other unexplained chronic diseases such as long COVID and post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach can be extended to other unexplained chronic diseases such as long COVID and post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions suggest extension to other chronic unexplained diseases; wording differs slightly but substantive claim and certainty are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Uncontrolled blood sugar levels may result in complications, namely diabetic neuropathy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This causal claim about uncontrolled blood sugar causing neuropathy appears in the preprint but is not represented in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early detection of diabetic neuropathy can prevent the risk of diabetic ulcers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Early detection of diabetic neuropathy can prevent the risk of diabetic ulcers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The prevention benefit of early detection is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This research identified 25 articles that discussed early detection of diabetic neuropathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This research identified 20 articles that discussed early detection of diabetic neuropathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core claim (identifying relevant articles) is the same but the reported count changed from 25 to 20, a factual revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method for early detection of neuropathy includes using a neuropathy questionnaire and physical examination. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most frequently used neuropathy instruments are the vibration perception threshold (VPT) and the Michigan Neuropathy Screening Instrument (MNSI). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies particular instruments (VPT, MNSI) consistent with the preprint's general statement about questionnaires/exams, thereby clarifying and narrowing the methods described.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One chloride intracellular channel 1 (CLIC1) probe was significant in predicting cognitive status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only one probe for chloride intracellular channel 1 (CLIC1) was significant after correction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report that only one CLIC1 probe reached significance; the publication adds 'after correction' but the finding is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive accuracy of classifying cognitive status using blood RNA levels averaged 87.87%, compared to a baseline of 55.46%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining individually nonsignificant probes with p-values less than 0.1 resulted in an average predictive accuracy of 87.87% for classifying cognitive status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the method (combining nonsignificant probes) that produced the 87.87% accuracy; the baseline figure in the preprint is not reiterated but the core accuracy result is retained and clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CLIC1 levels alone were not sufficient to determine dementia status. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CLIC1 levels alone were not sufficient to predict dementia status and cannot be used alone in a clinical setting. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the limitation and adds an explicit clinical applicability statement, clarifying the preprint limitation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining individually suggestive, but nonsignificant, blood RNA levels can significantly improve diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patient features that do not individually predict cognitive status might still contribute to overall cognitive decline through interactions that can be elucidated through machine learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the same idea in more general, methodological terms (features interacting and ML), keeping the same conceptual conclusion about combining weak signals.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hawkes processes can be used to develop accurate models of COVID-19 transmission with spatial-temporal covariates. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hawkes processes can be used to develop accurate models of COVID-19 transmission with spatial-temporal covariates. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model estimates the dynamic reproduction number of COVID-19 using an EM algorithm based on Google mobility indices and demographic covariates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model estimates the dynamic reproduction number of the virus using an EM algorithm based on Google mobility indices and demographic covariates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The methodological/result statement is effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Hawkes process outperforms several benchmark models, including an ensemble approach and a SEIR-variant, in short-term forecasting tasks for COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Hawkes process outperforms several models currently used to track the pandemic, including an ensemble approach and an SEIR-variant. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report superior short-term forecasting performance versus comparable benchmark models; wording differences do not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study investigates the importance of various covariates and mobility indices for building forecasts of COVID-19 in the U.S. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study investigates which covariates and mobility indices are most important for building forecasts of COVID-19 in the U.S. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The investigation of covariate importance is present in both versions with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Symptoms alone are sufficient to predict PCR outcome with a mean average precision of 86%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Symptoms alone are sufficient to predict RT-PCR outcome with a mean average precision of 86%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about symptom-only prediction performance is the same; publication uses 'RT-PCR' terminology but the result is identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combinations of symptoms can predict PCR positivity with 90% accuracy for anosmia/ageusia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combinations of symptoms can predict RT-PCR positivity with 90% accuracy for anosmia/ageusia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific symptom-combination accuracy is unchanged; only PCR terminology is updated to RT-PCR.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 30% of a subgroup with cardiopulmonary symptoms tested positive for PCR. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RT-PCR provides little added diagnostic value for a subgroup with cardiopulmonary symptoms, with only 30% of RT-PCR+ results. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical result (30%) is the same but the publication frames it as evidence about RT-PCR's limited added value for this subgroup, clarifying interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PCR provides little added diagnostic value based on clinical presentation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RT-PCR provides little added diagnostic value for a subgroup with cardiopulmonary symptoms, with only 30% of RT-PCR+ results. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general conclusion about PCR's limited value is revised in the publication to a narrower, subgroup-specific statement and is presented with higher certainty; overall the generality is reduced (narrowed) and the expression of confidence/claim shifts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A prescribing strategy based on clinical presentation can improve the global efficiency of PCR testing. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A prescribing strategy based on clinical presentation can improve the global efficiency of RT-PCR testing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions propose a clinically based prescribing strategy to improve testing efficiency; wording differences (PCR vs RT-PCR) do not change the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variance explained by the models based on Google search data were 87.7%, 79.1% and 52.0% for the whole country, the Littoral and Centre regions respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Random forest (RF) and support vector machines (SVM) had the highest average R2 (0.78 and 0.88, respectively) for predicting influenza-like illness (ILI) per 100,000 persons at the country level.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes model performance in terms of method-specific average R2 at the country level rather than presenting the exact regional variance-explained percentages from the preprint, narrowing the geographic/detail scope while keeping the core result about high R2s.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study demonstrates the need for developing contextualized approaches when using digital data for disease surveillance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates the need for developing contextualized approaches when using digital data for disease surveillance.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion verbatim, with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study demonstrates the potential usefulness of search data for monitoring ILI in sub-Saharan African countries.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study indicates the usefulness of search data for monitoring ILI in sub-Saharan African countries.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a slightly more cautious/less strong wording ("indicates" and lower stated certainty) compared with the preprint's stronger claim of "potential usefulness," so the claim is clarified and its confidence is tempered.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The R2 and RMSE were statistically similar across most of the methods applied in the study.
alignments.rationale: This methodological comparison (similarity of R2/RMSE across methods) was not present among the preprint claims and appears as a new/result-level claim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Asymptomatic COVID-19 patients exhibit highly activated anti-virus interferon and T/NK cell activation.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this specific result about interferon and T/NK activation in asymptomatic patients; no matching statement is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cytokine mRNAs in asymptomatic patients are effectively destabilized by abundant RNA binding proteins, preserving normal cytokine levels.
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic claim about RNA binding proteins destabilizing cytokine mRNAs is not represented in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Critically ill COVID-19 patients experience T/NK cell dysfunction due to cytokine storm caused by RNA binding proteins inhibition and tryptophan metabolites accumulation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neutrophils over-activation, arginine depletion, and tryptophan metabolites accumulation correlate with T cell dysfunction in critical COVID-19 patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a correlational link (neutrophil over-activation and metabolite accumulation correlating with T cell dysfunction) rather than the preprint's stronger causal mechanism invoking RNA binding protein inhibition and cytokine storm; the claim is therefore revised and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine-learning model was developed that accurately stratifies COVID-19 severities based on multi-omics features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reports a trans-omics landscape for COVID-19 based on integrative analysis of genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, and lipidomic profiles.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the integrative trans-omics landscape produced by the study rather than focusing on the ML-stratification tool highlighted in the preprint; the contribution is reframed/clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis provides insights into COVID-19 pathogenesis and identifies potential targets for treatment intervention.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multi-omics data and characterization of peripheral blood from COVID-19 patients may provide clues regarding the pathophysiology of and potential therapeutic strategies for COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses this as a more cautious, exploratory conclusion ("may provide clues") with lower stated certainty, so the claim is preserved but tempered/clarified.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Neutrophils heterogeneity is found between asymptomatic and critically ill COVID-19 patients.
alignments.rationale: The specific finding of neutrophil heterogeneity between clinical groups is presented in the publication but is not represented among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SPARRA v 4 is a predictive score for emergency admission risk that will be deployed nationwide in Scotland.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SPARRAv4 is a predictive score for emergency admission risk that will be deployed nationwide in Scotland.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution (deployment of SPARRAv4 nationwide) with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SPARRA v 4 demonstrates improvements in discrimination and calibration compared to previous scores deployed in Scotland.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SPARRAv4 demonstrates improvements in discrimination and calibration compared to previous scores deployed in Scotland.
alignments.rationale: The result about improved discrimination and calibration is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance of SPARRA v 4 varies across different population sub-groups and reasons for admission.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis provides insights about the epidemiology of emergency admission risk in Scotland by studying predictive performance across different population sub-groups.
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe variation in predictive performance across subgroups and use this to inform epidemiological insights; the publication reframes this slightly but preserves meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis provides insights into the epidemiology of emergency admission risk in Scotland.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis provides insights about the epidemiology of emergency admission risk in Scotland by studying predictive performance across different population sub-groups.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly ties epidemiological insights to subgroup analyses, which matches the preprint's general claim of providing insights into epidemiology.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study discusses broader challenges including reproducibility and the safe updating of risk prediction models at the population level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research discusses broader challenges including reproducibility and the safe updating of risk prediction models already deployed at the population level.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same limitation discussion about reproducibility and safe updating of deployed population-level models.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study quantifies the effect of individual input features on emergency admission risk.
alignments.rationale: Quantification of individual feature effects is presented in the publication but was not stated among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identify three unique transcriptomic programmes in keratinocytes, KC1, KC2, KC17, characteristic to immune signalling from disease-associated helper T cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identify three unique transcriptomic programmes in keratinocytes - KC1, KC2 and KC17 - characteristic of immune signalling from disease-associated Th cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The keratinocyte response during treatment is therapy dependent.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The keratinocyte response during treatment is therapy dependent.
alignments.rationale: Identical claim retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Broad spectrum treatment with ciclosporin ameliorated the KC17 response in AD lesions to a non-lesional immunophenotype, without altering KC2.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Broad-spectrum treatment with ciclosporin ameliorated the KC17 response in AD lesions to a nonlesional immunophenotype, without altering KC2.
alignments.rationale: The specific treatment effect on KC17 (and lack of KC2 effect) is presented unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The specific anti-Th2 therapy, dupilumab, reversed the KC2 immunophenotype.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The specific anti-Th2 therapy, dupilumab, reversed the KC2 immunophenotype.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same treatment-specific finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The perturbation of a single axis of immune signal alone may be insufficient to resolve keratinocyte immunophenotype abnormalities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The perturbation of a single axis of immune signal alone may be insufficient to resolve keratinocyte immunophenotype abnormalities.
alignments.rationale: The concluding insight about single-axis perturbation insufficiency is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model can predict critical outcomes in patients with SARS-CoV-2 using demographic variables, blood tests, pre-existing comorbidities, and CT-based radiomic features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model can identify SARS-CoV-2 patients at risk of serious illness and death based on early hospitalization data.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core conclusion that an ML model can identify at-risk patients but frames it more generally as using early hospitalization data (less explicit about CT radiomics), slightly broadening the data description while clarifying scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The strongest risk factors for mortality in SARS-CoV-2 patients were identified as D-dimer, age, and cardiovascular disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The strongest risk factors for death in SARS-CoV-2 patients were identified as D-dimer, age, and cardiovascular disease.
alignments.rationale: The key risk-factor finding is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model classified 97% of non-survivors as high-risk individuals, demonstrating its effectiveness in risk stratification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model classified 90% of non-survivors as high-risk individuals in the testing dataset.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a lower classification rate (90% vs 97%) and specifies the testing dataset, reducing the numerical performance claim and tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Median survival for high-risk individuals was 14 days, which was not significantly different from the 12 days observed in non-survivors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The estimated median survival in the high-risk group was 9 days, while the low-risk group did not reach the median survival of 50%.
alignments.rationale: The survival estimates differ substantially between versions (14 vs 9 days) and the publication reframes comparisons between high- and low-risk groups rather than comparing to non-survivors, so the claim is revised and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 73 risk factors of pancreatic cancer with the Phenome-wide Association Study (PheWAS) on a matched case-control cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 73 risk factors of pancreatic cancer with the Phenome-wide Association Study (PheWAS) on a matched case–control cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Texts are effectively identical in result and certainty, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model showed an AUROC at 0.742 [0.727, 0.757] which was similar in both the general population and in a subset of the population who has had prior cross-sectional imaging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model showed an AUROC at 0.742 [0.727, 0.757], which was similar in both the general population and in a subset of the population who has had prior cross-sectional imaging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and metrics are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prevalence of pancreatic cancer in those in the top 5 percentile of the risk score was 6 folds higher than the general population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rate of diagnosis of pancreatic cancer in those in the top 1 percentile of the risk score was 6 folds higher than the general population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The fold-increase is preserved but the percentile changed from top 5% to top 1%, which is a substantive revision that narrows the scope of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model leverages data extracted from a 6-month window of time in the electronic health record to identify patients at nearly 6-fold higher than baseline risk of developing pancreatic cancer 2.5 to 3 years from evaluation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model leverages data extracted from a 6-month window of time in the electronic health record to identify patients at nearly sixfold higher than baseline risk of developing pancreatic cancer 2.5–3 years from evaluation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content is the same but publication reports lower certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning pipeline yielded a final scoring system that included weight-related diagnosis codes, age, and number of days hospitalized and distinct drugs dispensed in the past 6 months. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning pipeline created a scoring system, B3S3, that predicts pre-operative BMI using claims data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the result as the named B3S3 predictor using claims data; features listed in preprint are less detailed in the publication, so the claim is clarified/packaged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The B3S3 had excellent performance in predicting pre-operative BMI with an R² of 0.862 in concurrent test data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The B3S3 demonstrated excellent performance in predicting pre-operative BMI with an R² of 0.862 in concurrent test data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical performance metric and phrasing between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The benchmark algorithm had good performance with an R² of 0.750, but it was inferior to the B3S3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The benchmark algorithm showed good performance with an R² of 0.750, but was inferior to the B3S3. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Content is consistent but the publication reports lower certainty (medium vs high), indicating tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The B3S3 is an accessible tool that can enhance confounding control and investigation of effect modification by baseline obesity levels in bariatric surgery studies utilizing claims data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The B3S3 is an accessible tool that can enhance confounding control and investigation of effect modification by baseline obesity levels in bariatric surgery studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim content is the same but publication assigns higher certainty (high vs medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification models performed well for most codes in improving the specificity of unspecific condition codes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification models performed well for most codes in improving the specificity of unspecific condition codes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is essentially identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed models improved the specificity of over half of all the unspecific code occurrences in the database. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed models improved the specificity of over half of all the unspecific code occurrences in the database. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings demonstrate the feasibility of using information from clinical text to enhance the specificity of unspecific condition codes in observational healthcare databases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings demonstrate the feasibility of using clinical text information to enhance the specificity of unspecific condition codes in healthcare databases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Substantively identical conclusion across versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Class imbalance and limited training data affected the performance of the (semi-)supervised models. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This limitation noted in the preprint does not appear in the publication text or claims, indicating removal.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future work could investigate transfer learning and validation of models across healthcare settings. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking suggestion in the preprint is not represented in the publication claims, so it appears removed.

alignments.pub_claim_text: No single classification approach consistently outperformed the others, indicating variability in performance across different methods. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication includes a new claim about variability across methods that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The improved level of detail from refined codes can benefit observational research and patient care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds a claim about downstream benefits for research and care that does not appear in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) achieve a non-linear mapping of data into a low-dimensional latent space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Variational autoencoders achieve a non-linear mapping of data into a low-dimensional latent space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical technical claim in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach allows for individualized, dynamic modeling in the latent space using ordinary differential equations (ODEs). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach allows for individualized, dynamic modeling in the latent space using ordinary differential equations (ODEs). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Same methodological contribution described in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel technique developed can obtain person-specific ODE parameters even with small sample sizes and incomplete data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel technique developed can obtain person-specific ODE parameters even with small sample sizes and incomplete data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result is presented the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method enables the investigation of individual reactions to stimuli, linking baseline characteristics to individual responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method enables the investigation of individual reactions to stimuli, linking baseline characteristics to individual responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion matches between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The promising results in the resilience application suggest that the dynamic deep learning approach may be useful in other application domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The promising results in the resilience application suggest that the dynamic deep learning approach may be useful in other application domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Application-generalization claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed CoAtNet model outperformed the EfficientNetV2 and ResNext50 models in classifying bone marrow cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed CoAtNet model outperformed the EfficientNetV2 and ResNext50 models in classifying bone marrow cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data augmentation techniques were effectively used to resolve class imbalances in the dataset. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data augmentation was effectively used to resolve class imbalances in the bone marrow cell categorization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Content is the same but publication reports lower certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The attention network property of the CoAtNet model increased the learning curve for the algorithm. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The attention network property of the CoAtNet model increased the learning curve for the algorithm. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim content is the same but publication indicates higher certainty (high vs medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SmoothGrad and Grad-CAM were used to analyze the CoAtNet model for explainability. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SmoothGrad and Grad-CAM were used to analyze the CoAtNet model for explainability. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Explainability methods are described the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Measurements of antibody responses and seroprevalence were consistent with the 3 sites’ known endemicity status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measurements of antibody responses and seroprevalence were consistent with the 3 sites' known endemicity status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with essentially identical wording and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning approach using 4 serological markers gave better predictions for P. falciparum cases in Palawan than individual antigen seropositivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning approach using 4 serological markers gave better predictions for Plasmodium falciparum recent infection in Palawan than individual antigen seropositivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies “recent infection” rather than the broader term “cases,” which clarifies the outcome being predicted while keeping the claim otherwise the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning classifications confirmed the absence of recent exposure to P. falciparum and P. vivax in both Occidental Mindoro and Bataan. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning classifications confirmed the absence of recent exposure to Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax in both Occidental Mindoro and Bataan. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about absence of recent exposure for both species in the two sites with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Seroprevalence and seroconversion rates based on cumulative exposure markers showed reliable trends in predicting historical P. falciparum and P. vivax transmission. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Seroprevalence and seroconversion rates based on cumulative exposure markers showed reliable trends in predicting historical Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax transmission. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same finding using nearly identical language and certainty, so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study emphasizes the utility of serological markers and machine learning models in assessing malaria transmission status in elimination settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study emphasizes the utility of serological markers in predicting recent and historical exposure in a sub-national elimination setting. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains emphasis on serological markers but drops explicit mention of machine-learning models and reframes the utility toward predicting recent and historical exposure at sub-national scale, changing the claim's focus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A set of acoustic parameters of cough sounds is relevant for the differentiation between COVID-19 positive and COVID-19 negative cough samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A set of acoustic parameters of cough sounds bears essential acoustic information for differentiating between COVID-19 positive and COVID-19 negative cough samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same result with synonymous wording and equivalent certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automatic COVID-19 detection model performs significantly above chance level, achieving an unweighted average recall (UAR) of 0.632. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The general automatic COVID-19 detection model performs significantly above chance level, achieving an unweighted average recall (UAR) of 0.632. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance and statistical claim are identical in the publication, so this is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning method relies on acoustic features showing higher effects in conventional group difference testing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Several acoustic features that show higher effects in conventional group difference testing are also higher weighted in the machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the linkage between group-difference features and model weighting more explicit, clarifying the original conclusion without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Whole-brain neural activity organizes into a limited range of states, suggesting a low-dimensional representation may capture its macroscale dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: UM-EEG presents a novel and physiologically meaningful representation to characterize brain states along the health-disease continuum. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the low-dimensional representation more strongly as a novel and physiologically meaningful tool across health-disease space, increasing confidence relative to the preprint's tentative wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The universal map of EEG (UM-EEG) captures meaningful distances and transitions between EEG states, demonstrating high semantic confinement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: UM-EEG presents a novel and physiologically meaningful representation to characterize brain states along the health-disease continuum. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication summarizes the technical description as a physiologically meaningful representation; this clarifies and packages the preprint's technical claim without reducing its intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Long-term EEG trajectories in the continuous embedding space can predict outcomes after cardiac arrest with an AUROC of 0.86. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: UM-EEG enables outcome prediction after cardiac arrest with an AUROC of 0.86. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative prognostic result verbatim, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The UM-EEG map identifies interpretable factors that govern prognosis, such as distance to healthy states and variability of state transitions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: UM-EEG identifies interpretable factors governing prognosis such as the distance to healthy states over time. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the interpretable prognostic factors (noting distance to healthy states over time), clarifying the original conclusion while preserving its meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: UM-EEG enhances current diagnostic and prognostic capabilities in clinical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification of out-of-sample EEG matches state-of-the-art artificial intelligence algorithms while extending it to the currently largest set of classes across the health-disease continuum. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete evidence of enhanced capability by reporting state-of-the-art classification performance and broader class coverage, which clarifies and supports the preprint's general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel, fully automatic and interpretable approach using deep convolutional neural networks can identify the design of nine different THR femoral implants from plain radiographs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural network (CNN) achieved the same or higher performance than at least one of the surgeons in identifying eight of nine THR implant designs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the original broad claim by reporting performance relative to surgeons and reducing the count from nine to eight designs where parity/higher performance was achieved, narrowing and revising the original contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN achieved on-par accuracy with three board-certified and fellowship trained orthopaedic surgeons while being significantly faster. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural network (CNN) achieved the same or higher performance than at least one of the surgeons in identifying eight of nine THR implant designs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more nuanced performance comparison (parity with at least one surgeon for eight of nine designs) than the preprint's statement of on-par accuracy with three specific surgeons, thus revising and narrowing the accuracy claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The need for additional training data for less distinct designs was highlighted. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN underperformed all of the surgeons in identifying one THR implant design (Anthology). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the limitation by identifying the specific design where the model underperformed, clarifying the preprint's general note about needing more data for less distinct designs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN can automatically identify the design of a failed THR femoral implant preoperatively in just a fraction of a second, saving time and improving identification accuracy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNNs can be used to automatically identify the design of a failed THR femoral implant preoperatively, potentially saving time and improving identification accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication tones the strong certainty of the preprint to a more cautious, potential-oriented conclusion and lowers stated certainty, so the claim is clarified and its confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Standard automated perimetry (SAP) suffers from practical issues that limit its effectiveness in some clinical populations. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both computational methods proposed in this study show potential for a more patient-friendly alternative to Standard Automated Perimetry. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the limitation into a positive conclusion about the proposed methods offering a patient-friendly alternative, which clarifies the implication of the preprint's limitation but with more cautious certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: New approaches to perimetry based on tracking eye movements attempt to mimic SAP but do not fully exploit the high spatial and temporal resolution of eye-tracking. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel eye-tracking method introduced in this study allows for high-resolution perimetry. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a novel method that does exploit high-resolution eye-tracking, which revises the preprint's more critical statement about prior approaches and strengthens the claim with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study presents two novel computational methods that utilize continuous gaze-tracking data to reconstruct visual field maps. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both computational methods proposed in this study show potential for a more patient-friendly alternative to Standard Automated Perimetry. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the potential application of the two methods presented in the preprint, clarifying their intended impact while expressing somewhat more cautious certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One of the methods introduced is based on the spatio-temporal integration of positional deviations using Threshold Free Cluster Enhancement (TFCE). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The TFCE algorithm's output significantly correlates with Standard Automated Perimetry performed with the Humphrey Field Analyzer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a concrete validation result for the TFCE method described in the preprint, clarifying the method's performance by demonstrating significant correlation with SAP.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The second method introduced involves training a recursive deep artificial neural network (RNN). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RNN accuracy significantly outperformed the TFCE in reconstructing simulated scotomas. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides empirical results showing the RNN's superiority over TFCE described in the preprint, clarifying the relative performance of the introduced methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Almost half of malaria infections are observed in pregnant women and children under 5 years, but children between 12 and 59 months carry the highest burden. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: More than half of malaria infections are observed in pregnant women and children under 5 years, with a high burden on children between 12 and 59 months. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same demographic pattern but adjusts the quantification from 'almost half' to 'more than half', a slight strengthening/clarification of the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network model performed better in predicting total malaria cases compared to the generalized linear model (GLM). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The artificial neural network (ANN) model performed better in predicting total malaria cases compared to the generalized linear model (GLM). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result (ANN outperforming GLM) with equivalent confidence and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generalized linear model (GLM) provides important information on the effect of predictors, which is valuable for mainstreaming target interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit claim about the GLM's role in providing interpretable predictor effects or its utility for mainstreaming interventions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early prediction of malaria cases can provide timely information needed to be proactive for intervention strategies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Early prediction of malaria cases can provide timely information needed for proactive intervention strategies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about the value of early prediction for proactive interventions with unchanged scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Openwater instrument had 79% sensitivity and 84% specificity for the detection of large vessel occlusion (LVO). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Openwater instrument had 79% sensitivity and 84% specificity for the detection of large vessel occlusion (LVO). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance metrics for sensitivity and specificity, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Openwater classification achieved an AUC of 0.82, outperforming the RACE scale and LAMS in detecting LVO. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The binary Openwater classification had an area under the ROC (AUROC) of 0.82, which outperformed the RACE scale and LAMS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same AUC and comparative performance with no substantive alteration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Openwater optical blood flow monitor outperformed prehospital stroke scales for the detection of LVO in patients presenting for acute stroke evaluation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Openwater optical blood flow monitor outperformed prehospital stroke scales for the detection of LVO in patients undergoing acute stroke evaluation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion that Openwater outperforms prehospital scales is carried forward unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings regarding the Openwater instrument need to be validated in the prehospital environment. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings need to be validated in an independent test set and the prehospital environment. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the limitation by adding the need for an independent test set in addition to prehospital validation, clarifying but not contradicting the original limitation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning-based risk scores outperformed a widely-used rule-based triage algorithm and human prioritization decisions in predicting hospital outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning-based risk scores outperformed a widely-used rule-based triage algorithm and human prioritization decisions in predicting hospital outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same primary result that ML risk scores outperform rule-based and human triage with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Concordance indexes for risk scores based on dispatch data ranged from 0.71 to 0.80, while those including ambulance data ranged from 0.79 to 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Concordance indexes for risk scores based on dispatch data ranged from 0.70-0.79, while those including ambulance data ranged from 0.79-0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports slightly adjusted concordance ranges (minor numeric revision) but retains the same comparative pattern and interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model performance in the prospective test dataset was similar to that found using cross-validation, and calibration was comparable to that of NEWS scores. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model performance in the prospective test dataset was similar to that found using cross-validation, and calibration was comparable to that of NEWS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The substantive claim about similar performance is preserved, but the publication presents it with stronger certainty (medium -> high), indicating amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research should investigate the generality of these results to prehospital triage in other settings. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future research should explore the robustness of these methods when applied to other settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the call for external evaluation but frames it as assessing robustness and expresses it with higher certainty (low -> medium), amplifying the stated need.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning algorithm was developed to quantify liver fat with correlation coefficients of 0.97 and 0.99 in hold-out testing datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The abdominal MRI-based machine-learning algorithm accurately estimates liver fat with correlation coefficients between 0.97 and 0.99. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the algorithm's high correlation in hold-out testing with essentially the same metrics and interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among 36,703 individuals with abdominal MRI imaging, 17% met the criteria for hepatic steatosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 17% of participants had predicted liver fat levels indicative of steatosis, which could not have been reliably estimated based on clinical factors such as BMI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 17% prevalence claim but adds that these cases could not be reliably identified using clinical measures, providing additional context/clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A polygenic score integrating information from eight genetic variants was strongly associated with future clinical diagnosis of liver diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A polygenic score integrating eight genetic variants was strongly associated with future risk of chronic liver disease, with a hazard ratio greater than 1.32 per SD score. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more specific outcome phrasing ('chronic liver disease') and quantifies the association (HR >1.32/SD), clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inactivating variants in the APOB gene were associated with substantially lower LDL cholesterol but more than 10-fold increased risk of steatosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
