alignments.pub_claim_text: Rare inactivating variants in the APOB or MTTP genes were identified in 0.8% of individuals with steatosis and conferred more than 6-fold risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication revises the genetic finding: it expands to include MTTP, reports prevalence, and lowers the reported risk magnitude (from >10-fold to >6-fold), thus changing effect size and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random forest model achieved an accuracy of 69.8% in predicting specific causes of death in lung cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction accuracy of the tuned RF model was 69.8%, compared to 64.6% for the MLR model using a 1:1 sample-split. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same 69.8% prediction accuracy, situating it alongside the comparison to MLR; the core accuracy claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final random forest model outperformed the multinomial regression model in accuracy, with 69.8% compared to 64.6%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tuned random forests (RF) model outperformed the multinomial logistic regression (MLR) model in predicting causes of death among lung cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same comparative result that the tuned RF outperformed MLR, matching the preprint's performance comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The top-10 most important factors in the random forest model included sex, chemotherapy status, age, radiotherapy status, nodal status, T category, histology type, and laterality. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The top-10 important factors in the RF model included sex, chemotherapy status, age, radiotherapy status, nodal status, T category, histology type, and laterality. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same top predictors, preserving the contribution claim with similar emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random forest model was optimized to predict specific causes of death in lung cancer patients using clinicopathologic factors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tuned random forests (RF) model outperformed the multinomial logistic regression (MLR) model in predicting causes of death among lung cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes a 'tuned' RF that outperformed MLR, which supports the preprint's claim about optimization using clinicopathologic factors but is less explicit about the optimization process; thus it is a clarification rather than a direct restatement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a predictive model that enables oversight of Adverse Event (AE) reporting for clinical Quality Program Leads (QPL). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A predictive model was developed to enable oversight of adverse event reporting for clinical quality program leads. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We built a model that infers the site reporting behavior from patient-level observations and compares them across a study to enable a robust detection of outliers between clinical sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The new model infers site reporting behavior from patient-level observations and detects outliers between clinical sites. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same result—inferring site behavior and detecting outliers—without changing scope or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new model will enhance further quality assurance activities for safety reporting from clinical trials and generate quality evidence during pre-approval inspections. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model enhances further quality assurance activities for safety reporting from clinical trials. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the enhancement claim but omits the specific assertion about generating evidence for pre-approval inspections, narrowing the original implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This model complements our previously published machine learning approach and is used by clinical quality professionals to better detect safety underreporting. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention complementing a prior approach or explicit uptake by clinical quality professionals, so this specific contribution is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final XGBoost model achieved an AUC of 80.5% on the held-out test set, indicating high predictive accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final XGBoost model achieved an area under the receiver operating curve of 80.5% and an area under precision-recall curve of 0.093 on the test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUC and adds the precision-recall metric, clarifying model performance without changing the claim's substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model's top 5% of risk predictions captured 37.4% of those presented with an ACSC-related hospitalization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predictions captured 37.4% of those presented with an ACSC-related hospitalisation when ranking the top 5% of risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numerical result and framing are presented in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model identified various contributing features to ACSC hospitalization risk, including previous ambulatory care visits and rural residence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the same list of contributing features or specific predictors, so this preprint claim is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model was able to capture the geospatial heterogeneity of ACSC risk in Ontario, highlighting elevated risk in rural and marginalized regions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model was able to capture the geospatial heterogeneity of ACSC risk in Ontario, particularly in rural and marginalised regions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the geospatial heterogeneity finding with the same regional emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the potential of machine learning to inform population health planning and interventions for reducing ACSC-related hospitalizations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates the potential of the model to inform population health planning and interventions to reduce the burden of ACSC-related hospitalisations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about potential utility for population health planning and interventions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Geospatial machine learning techniques can predict the location of safe and unsafe areas of groundwater arsenic contamination. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A habitation-level predictive model was developed to determine the risk and extent of arsenic concentrations greater than 10 μg/L in groundwater. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both describe development of a geospatial predictive model to determine areas at risk of arsenic contamination; wording differs but meaning aligns.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified extensive areas of potential arsenic hotspots based on a probability of ≥0.7 for arsenic concentrations greater than 10 µg/L. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest model predicted probabilities of arsenic at concentrations greater than 10 μg/L using intrinsic and extrinsic predictor variables. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the probabilistic predictions and model type but does not restate the specific ≥0.7 hotspot threshold; thus the result is presented in a more general/model-focused way.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Twenty-five percent of the land area (1,500 km²) was identified as a high-risk zone for arsenic contamination, affecting an estimated population of 155,000. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 25% of the total land area was identified as a high-risk zone for arsenic contamination, potentially affecting 155,000 people. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same quantitative area and population-at-risk estimate.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ternary hazard map developed in this study can inform policymakers for targeted interventions in regions with high arsenic risk. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ternary hazard probability map can inform policymakers on establishing new drinking water treatment plants and providing safe drinking water connections to rural households. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the policymaker-use claim but specifies actionable interventions, clarifying the intended policy uses.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning (ML)-based prediction model studies adhered poorly to the TRIPOD statement, the current standard reporting guideline. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The completeness of reporting in Machine Learning-based prediction model studies is poor, similar to that of conventional regression-based techniques. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions make the same overall claim that completeness/adherence is poor; the publication frames it in comparison to regression-based techniques but preserves the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall, articles adhered to a median of 38.7% of TRIPOD items. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine Learning-based prediction model studies adhere to a median of 38.7% of TRIPOD items. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical quantitative result presented in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Essential information to decide to use the model, such as model specification and its performance, is rarely reported. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No article fully adhered to complete reporting of the abstract, and very few reported essential elements such as the flow of participants and model specification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete examples of the 'rarely reported' elements mentioned in the preprint, clarifying the nature of missing information.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An extension of TRIPOD for ML-based prediction model studies is needed. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some items and sub-items of TRIPOD might be less suitable for Machine Learning-based prediction model studies, indicating a need for extensions to TRIPOD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens and specifies the rationale—some items may be less suitable—while preserving the conclusion that extensions are needed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is an urgent need to improve the reporting quality and usability of research to avoid research waste. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is an urgent need to improve the reporting quality and usability of research to avoid research waste. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same urgent recommendation with comparable certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of the included studies reported high usability, acceptability, and engagement of AI-chatbots. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There were mixed results regarding the feasibility, acceptability, and usability of AI chatbots. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the characterization from 'majority high' to 'mixed results', which revises and tempers the original positive summary and narrows the claim's certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Selected studies demonstrated high efficacy in promoting healthy lifestyles, smoking cessation, treatment adherence, and reduction in substance misuse. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI chatbots demonstrated high efficacy in promoting healthy lifestyles, smoking cessation, treatment adherence, and reduction in substance misuse. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the efficacy claim more broadly (dropping 'selected studies'), which strengthens the presentation of efficacy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI-chatbots have demonstrated potential for scalability by deployment through accessible devices and platforms. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI chatbots have the potential for scalability by being deployed through accessible devices and platforms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the scalability conclusion but expresses it with higher certainty than the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants reported that AI-chatbots offered a non-judgmental space for communicating sensitive information. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants reported that AI chatbots provided a nonjudgmental space for communicating sensitive information. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The participant-reported nonjudgmental space is stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reported results need to be interpreted with caution due to moderate to high risk of internal validity and limitations for generalizability. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future studies need to adopt robust randomized control trials to establish definitive conclusions about AI chatbots. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts from a general caution about validity and generalizability to a specific recommendation for RCTs, revising and narrowing the original limitation statement and emphasizing caution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed edge AI system can detect and differentiate filaria species in real time using a smartphone camera aligned with an optical microscope. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed edge AI system can detect and differentiate filaria species in real time using a smartphone camera aligned with an optical microscope. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The object detection algorithm achieved an overall precision of 94.14%, recall of 91.90%, and F1 score of 93.01% for the screening algorithm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The object detection algorithm achieved an overall precision of 94.14%, recall of 91.90%, and F1 score of 93.01% for the screening algorithm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical screening algorithm performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system can distinguish four species of microfilarias at 40x magnification: Loa loa, Mansonella perstans, Wuchereria bancrofti, and Brugia malayi. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The species differentiation algorithm achieved precision, recall, and F1 scores of 95.46%, 97.81%, and 96.62%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides explicit performance metrics for species differentiation (clarifying the preprint species-differentiation claim) though it omits the species list in this sentence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This innovative solution has the potential to support filariasis diagnosis and monitoring in resource-limited settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This AI-assisted diagnostic solution has the potential to support filariasis diagnosis and monitoring in resource-limited settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion with minor wording change ('AI-assisted').

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single nucleotide polymorphisms collectively associate with baseline cognitive performance in a large ALS patient cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single nucleotide polymorphisms collectively associate with baseline cognitive performance in a large ALS patient cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A polygenic risk score derived using sCCA relates to longitudinal cognitive decline in ALS patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A polygenic risk score derived using sCCA relates to longitudinal cognitive decline in ALS patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same sCCA-derived PRS association with longitudinal decline.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The polygenic risk score is associated with in vivo cortical thinning in multiple brain regions in ALS patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The polygenic risk score is associated with in vivo cortical thinning in multiple brain regions and post-mortem motor cortical neuronal loss in ALS cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the in vivo cortical-thinning association and adds post-mortem neuronal-loss evidence, broadening and strengthening the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Common genetic polymorphisms may exert a polygenic contribution to the risk of cortical disease vulnerability and cognitive dysfunction in ALS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Common genetic polymorphisms may exert a polygenic contribution to the risk of cortical disease vulnerability and cognitive dysfunction in ALS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same concluding interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: XGBoost gradient boosted tree models achieved AUROC values of 0.905, 0.827, 0.810, and 0.790 for predicting ARDS at 0-, 12-, 24-, and 48-hour windows prior to onset, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGBoost gradient boosted tree models achieved an AUROC value of 0.905 for detecting ARDS at onset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication splits the multi-window result; the 0.905 onset AUROC directly corresponds to the preprint's 0-hour value (a clarification/splitting of the original combined claim).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the feasibility of using patient health record data for the early prediction of ARDS. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Supervised machine learning predictions may help predict patients with ARDS up to 48 hours prior to onset. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the implication more cautiously (may help predict up to 48 hours) rather than explicitly 'demonstrates feasibility,' so the claim is clarified and slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Supervised machine learning predictions may help predict patients with ARDS up to 48 hours prior to onset. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models predicted ARDS with AUROC values of 0.827, 0.810, and 0.790 for 12-, 24-, and 48-hour windows prior to onset, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the predictive performance for the 12–48 hour windows, directly supporting the preprint's conclusion about up-to-48-hour prediction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AUC of the model discriminating between group N and group I was 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AUC of the model discriminating between healthy subjects and IBS patients was 0.95. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUC for the N vs I (healthy vs IBS) discrimination.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value of group I detection were 30.8%, 97.6%, 66.7%, and 90.2%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sensitivity and specificity of the AI model for detecting Group I were 30.8% and 97.6%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same sensitivity and specificity but omits PPV and NPV, so the claim is narrowed/partially revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall AUC of the model discriminating between groups N, C, and D was 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall AUC of the model discriminating between Groups N, C, and D was 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this overall multi-group AUC result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prospective studies are needed to further validate whether this externally validated model has similar diagnostic capabilities at other facilities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prospective studies are needed to validate the diagnostic capabilities of the AI model at other facilities. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Both versions express the same limitation and call for prospective validation at other sites.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model can be used to determine treatment efficacy. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include or support the claim about using the model to determine treatment efficacy; that claim is absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The genetic correlation between retinal age gaps predicted from the UK Biobank and GoDARTs cohorts was found to be 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The genetic correlation between retinal age gaps predicted from the UK Biobank and GoDARTS cohorts was found to be 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this genetic-correlation result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thirteen retinal age gap loci were identified that may be related to retinal vessel density and other aging processes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Thirteen retinal age gap loci were identified that may be related to retinal vessel density and other aging processes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same loci-identification result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SNP-wide heritability of retinal age gap was determined to be 0.15. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SNP-wide heritability of retinal age gap was determined to be 0.15. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical heritability estimate.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Glycated hemoglobin, inflammation hemocytes, and anemia may be associated with accelerated retinal aging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Glycated hemoglobin, inflammation hemocytes, and anemia may be associated with accelerated retinal aging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same associations as possible contributors to accelerated retinal aging.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides potential pharmaceutical intervention targets for further treatment of aging processes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not claim or list potential pharmaceutical intervention targets; this forward-looking intervention claim is absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.92 on the internal test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning system achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.92 on the internal test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric result and dataset; wording change (AI -> deep learning system) does not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was no statistically significant difference in performance between the AI system and 5 board-certified breast radiologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was no statistically significant difference in performance between the deep learning system and five board-certified breast radiologists. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same core comparison and result, but publication reports a lower certainty level (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Averaging radiologists' predictions with the AI's predictions improved their performance and increased interreader agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim describes combining/averaging radiologists' and AI predictions or reporting improved interreader agreement; the claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system can reduce unnecessary biopsies in up to 20% of all BI-RADS category 4 patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning system can reduce unnecessary biopsies in the range of clinically relevant risk thresholds. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication drops the specific 20%/BI-RADS-4 framing and instead states a general reduction across clinically relevant thresholds, broadening and changing the specificity while increasing stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The exploratory work creates a foundation for deployment and prospective analysis of AI-based models for breast MRI. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning system demonstrated generalizability using multiple datasets from Poland and the United States. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete evidence (generalizability across datasets) that supports and narrows the preprint's broader claim about creating a foundation for deployment; certainty is higher in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was no appreciable difference in parameter estimates with vs. without last-dose time incorporated in the data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was no appreciable difference in parameter estimates with assumed versus extracted last-dose time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording differs slightly but the substantive result is the same: no appreciable difference in parameter estimates regarding last-dose time handling.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our sensitivity analysis revealed little difference between parameters estimated assuming a range of absorption rate constants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our sensitivity analysis revealed little difference between parameters estimated across a range of assumed absorption rate constants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same sensitivity-analysis finding with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Drugs with a slower elimination rate (or a longer half-life) are less sensitive to dose timing errors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Drugs with a slower elimination rate (or a longer half-life) are less sensitive to dose timing errors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical conclusion and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experimental designs which only allow for trough blood concentrations are usually insensitive to deviation in absorption rate. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental designs which only allow for trough blood concentrations are usually insensitive to deviation in absorption rate. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same design-related conclusion with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CovTransformer framework accurately forecasts SARS-CoV-2 lineage frequencies two months into the future in 31 countries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CovTransformer model makes accurate predictions two months into the future with high levels of accuracy both globally and at the US-state level. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports similar two-month forecasting accuracy but broadens geographic scope (global and US-state) relative to the preprint's 31-country statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CovTransformer outperforms the current gold-standard regression-based model implemented in Nextstrain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CovTransformer model performed substantially better than the multinomial regression model implemented in Nextstrain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same comparative result against the Nextstrain regression model, with equivalent strength language in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transformer models represent a promising approach for lineage forecasting and pandemic monitoring. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transformer models represent a promising approach for SARS-CoV-2 forecasting and pandemic monitoring. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is preserved but the publication reports a lower certainty level (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost model detected patients at high risk 2.7 years before T2D, achieving an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The XGBoost model detected patients at high risk of developing type 2 diabetes (T2D) 2.7 years before the onset of the disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The timing/detection aspect is identical; the AUROC detail is reported elsewhere in the publication (separate claim).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model can accurately predict development of T2D among patients with mental illness based on routinely collected data from electronic health records. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model can accurately predict the development of T2D among patients with mental illness based on routine clinical data from electronic health records. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same overall conclusion and data source; language is effectively equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study is the first to develop and validate a machine learning model for prediction of T2D among patients with mental illness. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert novelty/firstness; that specific contribution claim was not carried into the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed model is sensitive and specific, detecting patients at high risk using only routinely collected data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive performance of the XGBoost model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.84. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides an explicit performance metric (AUROC=0.84) that clarifies the preprint's more qualitative sensitivity/specificity claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Implementing such a system in clinical practice may inform targeted interventions to reduce the risk of T2D. (certainty=medium, type=implication)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A decision support system based on the machine learning model may inform measures to prevent the development of T2D in this high-risk population. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same implication about potential clinical decision support to inform prevention measures; wording differs but meaning is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT achieved 55.8% and 42.7% accuracy in two 260-question simulated exams. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The legacy model achieved 55.8% accuracy on the BCSC set and 42.7% on the OphthoQuestions set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact numeric accuracies on the two test sets are the same; publication labels the model as 'legacy' but the result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT's performance varied across subspecialties, with the best results in general medicine and the worst in neuro-ophthalmology and ophthalmic pathology and intraocular tumors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Logistic regression analysis of the legacy model showed that the examination section and question difficulty were most predictive of ChatGPT's answer accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides an analysis identifying predictors (section and difficulty) which clarifies the preprint's observed subspecialty variation rather than listing particular subspecialties.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest that specialising LLMs through domain-specific pre-training may be necessary to improve their performance in ophthalmic subspecialties. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Specializing LLMs through domain-specific pretraining may be necessary to improve their performance in ophthalmic subspecialties. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation is preserved verbatim but the publication expresses it with higher certainty (low -> medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pathological image based prognosis prediction using Cox-nnet is significantly more accurate than Cox-PH and random survival forests models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cox-nnet is significantly more accurate than Cox proportional hazards and Random Survival Forests models on pathological image features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claims are effectively identical in substance and context (pathological image features), only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-stage Cox-nnet complex model combining histopathology image and transcriptomics RNA-Seq data achieves better prognosis prediction, with a median C-index of 0.75 and log-rank p-value of 6e-7 in the testing datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-stage Cox-nnet model combining histopathology image and transcriptomic RNA-seq data achieves much better prognosis prediction than PAGE-Net. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication reframes the claim as a direct comparison to PAGE-Net and omits the specific numeric metrics reported in the preprint, changing the emphasis and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Imaging features present additional predictive information to gene expression features, as the combined model is much more accurate than the model with gene expression alone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Imaging features present additional predictive information to gene expression features, as the combined model is more accurate than the model with gene expression alone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result and interpretation about additive predictive information from imaging features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pathological image features are modestly correlated with gene expression. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pathological image features are correlated with gene expression, as genes correlated to top imaging features present known associations with HCC patient survival. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the claim by linking correlated genes to known survival associations, providing additional biological context beyond the preprint's modest-correlation statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work provides two-stage Cox-nnet, a new class of biologically relevant and relatively interpretable models, to integrate multi-modal and multiple types of data for survival prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work proposes two-stage Cox-nnet, a new class of biologically relevant and interpretable models, to integrate multiple types of heterogeneous data for survival prediction. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same high-level contribution but tones down certainty and slightly rephrases the description (heterogeneous data), reflecting a tempered claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Enhanced Scattering Wavelet Convolutional Neural Network (ESWCNN) improves polyp classification performance by combining Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Scattering Wavelet Transform (SWT). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Enhanced Scattering Wavelet Convolutional Neural Network (ESWCNN) improves polyp classification performance by combining Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Scattering Wavelet Transform (SWT). (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ESWCNN achieved a classification accuracy of 96.4% in three-class polyp classification and 94.8% in two-class polyp classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ESWCNN achieved a classification accuracy of 96.4% in three-class polyp classification and 94.8% in two-class polyp classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric performance results are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the three-class classification, correct classification rates of 96.2% for adenomas, 98.71% for hyperplastic polyps, and 97.9% for serrated polyps were achieved. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the three-class classification, the correct classification rates were 96.2% for adenomas, 98.71% for hyperplastic polyps, and 97.9% for serrated polyps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Per-class accuracy values are unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method reached an average sensitivity of 96.7% with 93.1% specificity in the two-class experiment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method reached an average sensitivity of 96.7% with 93.1% specificity in the two-class experiment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Sensitivity and specificity results are carried over unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed ESWCNN method can effectively classify polyps with higher accuracy and efficacy compared to state-of-the-art CNN models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed ESWCNN method can effectively classify polyps with higher accuracy and efficacy compared to state-of-the-art CNN models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The overall conclusion and comparative claim remain the same in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Aifred clinical decision support system was found to be acceptable to clinicians and feasible for use during clinical encounters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Aifred clinical decision support system was acceptable to clinicians and feasible for use during clinical encounters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the acceptability and feasibility conclusion with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clinicians expressed a significant degree of trust in the AI’s predictions to assist with treatment selection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinicians indicated a willingness to use the Aifred tool in real clinical practice and reported a significant degree of trust in the system's predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the clinicians' stated willingness to use the tool in practice while retaining the reported trust, expanding the claim's practical implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The simulation environment effectively allowed for the evaluation of the tool’s impact on the physician-patient interaction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The simulation environment allowed for the evaluation of the tool's impact on the physician-patient interaction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion about the simulation environment's suitability for evaluating interactions; certainty is described higher but the content is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results from the simulation study will inform further tool development and clinician training materials. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This forward-looking statement about informing further development and training materials is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates that predicting wait times from simple, readily available data is complex and provides estimates that aren’t as accurate as patients would like. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not reproduce the preprint's emphasis on complexity and patient dissatisfaction with accuracy; instead it frames predictive ability more positively, so this specific claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Important variables affecting wait times include triage category, last-k patient average wait time, and arrival time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not enumerate the same specific important predictor variables; that itemized result is not present in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Emergency medicine machine learning models may be less generalisable to other sites than expected, as models developed for one site lose accuracy at another site. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wait time prediction models are not transferable across hospitals. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication's limitation statement matches the preprint's conclusion about lack of generalisability across sites.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rolling average models under-estimate wait times, while random forest and linear regression models perform better. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Random forest and linear regression models outperformed other models in predicting wait times. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same performance ordering, emphasizing RF and linear regression superiority over simpler approaches.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Electronic emergency demographic and flow information can be used to approximate emergency patient wait times. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models can predict emergency patient wait times applicable to a variety of emergency departments. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the preprint's idea by stating ML models can predict wait times across EDs, broadening the applicability beyond the preprint's phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PD-L1 DLS significantly discriminated PD-L1 positive and negative patients with an AUC≥0.82 in all cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PD-L1 DLS significantly discriminated between PD-L1 positive and negative patients with an area under the receiver operating characteristics curve of ≥0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same discriminative performance (AUC≥0.82) with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher PD-L1 DLS was significantly associated with a higher probability of durable clinical benefit, longer progression-free survival, and longer overall survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DLS was indistinguishable from IHC-derived PD-L1 status in predicting progression-free survival and overall survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the association as equivalence to IHC-derived status in predicting outcomes rather than the preprint's direct association phrasing; emphasis and framing changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS combined with clinical characteristics achieved C-indices of 0.86, 0.83, and 0.81 for durable clinical benefit prediction in retrospective, prospective, and external cohorts, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A score generated by combining the DLS with clinical characteristics accurately predicted durable clinical benefit, progression-free survival, and overall survival with C-indexes of 0.70-0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a broader range of C-index values (0.70–0.87) instead of the exact cohort-specific values from the preprint, indicating revision and a tempered reporting of performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DLS provides a non-invasive and promising approach to predict PD-L1 expression and infer clinical outcomes for immunotherapy-treated NSCLC patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study proposes DLS as a surrogate for IHC-determined PD-L1 measurement to guide individual pretherapy decisions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the intended clinical application explicit (surrogate for IHC to guide decisions), clarifying and broadening the preprint's proposed non-invasive predictive use.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generated signature may serve as a prognostic biomarker for immunotherapy response in patients with NSCLC and outperforms clinical characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A score generated by combining the DLS with clinical characteristics accurately predicted durable clinical benefit, progression-free survival, and overall survival with C-indexes of 0.70-0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports combined-score predictive performance but does not support the preprint's stronger claim that the signature outright outperforms clinical characteristics; the claim is therefore revised and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The segmentation algorithm achieved high F1 scores of almost 1.0 for neurosensory retina and subretinal fluid.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel deep learning based semantic segmentation algorithm provides benchmark results for automatic segmentation of 11 OCT features in treatment naive patients undergoing anti-VEGF therapy.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution as benchmark results across 11 OCT features rather than reporting the near-1.0 F1 for specific features, broadening the scope and removing the specific extreme metric.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant changes occurred in intraretinal fluid, subretinal fluid, subretinal hyperreflective material, and fibrovascular PED after anti-VEGF therapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant changes occurred in intraretinal fluid, subretinal fluid, subretinal hyperreflective material, and fibrovascular PED after 3 and 12 months of anti-VEGF therapy.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the timepoints (3 and 12 months) for the significant changes, clarifying the preprint claim without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Anti-VEGF therapy provokes the most potent changes in OCT biomarkers during the first 3 months and subsequently acts only as a stabilizing agent.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anti-VEGF therapy provokes the most potent changes in OCT biomarkers during the first 3 months of treatment.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the strong early-effect claim but drops the explicit statement that therapy thereafter only stabilizes, narrowing and tempering the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A gradual loss of RPE hints at a progressing decline of visual acuity even beyond month 12.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding claim about RPE loss or its link to long-term visual acuity appears in the publication, so this claim was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The segmentation algorithm allows efficient volumetric analysis of OCT scans.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel deep learning based semantic segmentation algorithm provides benchmark results for automatic segmentation of 11 OCT features in treatment naive patients undergoing anti-VEGF therapy.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes benchmark automatic segmentation across multiple OCT features, which supports and clarifies the preprint's claim about enabling volumetric analysis though with different emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multitask deep learning model demonstrates a dice coefficient higher than 0.78 for segmentation of COVID-19 lesions from chest CT images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed multitask deep learning model demonstrates a dice coefficient higher than 0.88 for segmentation.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a substantially higher Dice score than the preprint, strengthening (amplifying) the segmentation performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieves an area under the ROC curve higher than 93% for the classification of COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieves an area under the ROC curve higher than 97% for classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports an improved AUC relative to the preprint, amplifying the classification performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The architecture of the model includes an encoder and two decoders for reconstruction and segmentation, along with a multi-layer perceptron for classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The architecture leverages information from multiple related tasks to improve segmentation and classification performances.
alignments.rationale: The publication describes the architecture's multitask rationale and benefits rather than listing specific modules, clarifying the functional intent of the preprint architecture description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multi-step semantic segmentation algorithm effectively analyzes individual arteries in invasive coronary angiography (ICA).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method effectively analyzes individual arteries in invasive coronary angiography.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about effective analysis of individual arteries, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm achieves an artery classification accuracy of 70.33% on a dataset of 225 ICAs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed multi-step semantic segmentation algorithm achieves a mean accuracy of 70.33% for multi-class artery classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same accuracy figure and task framing, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method utilizes a deep learning-based approach to extract the entire arterial binary mask from ICAs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrates a mean intersection over union of 0.6868 for semantic segmentation of arteries.
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies semantic segmentation performance (IoU), which clarifies and provides a concrete metric for the preprint's methodological claim about mask extraction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The morphological similarity among different types of arteries poses a challenge for deep-learning-based models in generating accurate semantic segmentation.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding limitation about morphological similarity appears in the publication claims, so this cautionary claim was not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models developed for analyzing MFC data in high-dimensional space are highly accurate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The workflow developed in this study increased the model's overall performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a specific outcome (increased overall performance) that concretizes the preprint's general statement about high accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current AI models for subtype classification based on MFC data are limited to the antibody panel they were trained on.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state this limitation as a claim; instead it focuses on transfer learning solutions, so the explicit limitation claim is not preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed workflow extends the previous model to four additional MFC panels using knowledge transfer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transfer learning can be applied to boost the performance of models with smaller datasets acquired with different MFC panels.
alignments.rationale: The publication generalizes the specific extension to a broader conclusion about applying transfer learning across different MFC panels, clarifying the preprint's methodological claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow increases the model’s overall performance and enhances the learning rate for very small training sizes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study improved the learning rate for small training sizes.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly reports improved learning rates for small training sizes, directly matching the preprint's claim about enhanced learning with small datasets.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PACT-3D achieved a sensitivity of 0.81 and a specificity of 0.99 in retrospective testing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieves a sensitivity of 0.81–0.83 and a specificity of 0.97–0.99 across retrospective, prospective, and external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication aggregates results across validations into ranges that include the preprint's retrospective numbers, clarifying the performance context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prospective validation of PACT-3D yielded a sensitivity of 0.83 and a specificity of 0.99.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieves a sensitivity of 0.81–0.83 and a specificity of 0.97–0.99 across retrospective, prospective, and external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports pooled ranges across validations that encompass the reported prospective figures, clarifying and situating the prospective results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sensitivity improved to 0.95 and 0.98 when excluding cases with a small amount of free air in simulated and prospective test sets, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sensitivity improves to 0.92–0.98 when cases with a small amount of free air (total volume <10 ml) are excluded.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a wider range for sensitivity after excluding small-volume cases, which clarifies and slightly broadens the preprint's specific two-number statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PACT-3D holds significant potential for assisting rapid decision-making in emergency care, thereby potentially improving patient outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The implementation of this model has the potential to accelerate diagnostic and treatment workflows in emergency care.
alignments.rationale: Both statements express the same conclusion about potential to accelerate emergency care workflows and improve clinical decision-making.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Digital Health-Enabled Community-Centered Care (D-CCC) represents a pioneering vision for the future of community-centered care. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Digital health-enabled community-centered care (D-CCC) represents a pioneering vision for the future of community-centered care. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim verbatim, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: D-CCC enables timely and individualized delivery of interventions by community health workers to the communities they serve. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: D-CCC enables more timely, supported, and individualized community health worker-delivered interventions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the original claim by adding that interventions are also "supported" and "more" timely, clarifying but not changing the underlying result or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: D-CCC has the potential to transform the current landscape of manual, episodic and restricted community health worker-delivered care and services. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: D-CCC has the potential to move community-centered care into an expanded, digitally interconnected, and collaborative community-centered health and social care ecosystem. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the potential impact from transforming CHW-delivered care to enabling a broader digitally interconnected ecosystem, shifting emphasis and broadening scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: D-CCC creates an expanded, digitally interconnected and collaborative community-centered health and social care ecosystem. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: D-CCC has the potential to move community-centered care into an expanded, digitally interconnected, and collaborative community-centered health and social care ecosystem. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this conclusion with near-identical wording and intent, leaving scope and confidence intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoGPT, a QLoRA fine-tuned Llama-2 model, outperformed other LLMs with win rates ranging from 87% to 99% in various automatic metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoGPT, a fine-tuned Llama-2 model, outperformed other models with win rates ranging from 87% to 99% in various automatic metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same quantitative performance result for the fine-tuned Llama-2 model, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoGPT produced reports comparable to cardiologists in qualitative review, significantly preferred in conciseness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoGPT produced reports comparable to cardiologists in qualitative review, being significantly preferred in conciseness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the qualitative finding verbatim, preserving scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future metrics for echo report quality should emphasize factual correctness, especially on numerical measurements. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Scalable evaluation approaches dedicated to echo reports remain necessary. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the need for scalable evaluation approaches rather than explicitly prioritizing factual/numeric correctness; the recommendation is reframed and therefore revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study evaluated multiple open-source LLMs and different model adaptation methods in echocardiography report summarization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoGPT, a fine-tuned Llama-2 model, outperformed other models with win rates ranging from 87% to 99% in various automatic metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the evaluation as producing a specific performance result (outperforming others), which clarifies the contribution statement about evaluating multiple models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The EchoGPT system demonstrated the potential of introducing LLMs into echocardiography practice to generate draft reports for human review and approval. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoGPT can generate draft reports for human review and approval, helping to streamline the workflow. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same practical application but states it with higher confidence and emphasizes workflow streamlining, amplifying the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: White matter hyperintensities (WMH) are an MRI-based biomarker of cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on a segmentation tool and performance metrics and does not restate the general biomarker claim about WMH.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: WMH are associated with accelerated cognitive decline and increased risk of stroke and dementia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims center on segmentation performance and do not mention clinical associations of WMH, so this epidemiologic claim is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: segcsvd WMH is a novel convolutional neural network-based tool for quantifying WMH. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: segcsvd_WMH demonstrated superior accuracy, achieving mean Dice score improvements of 7.8% ± 9.7% over HyperMapp3r, 21.8% ± 8.6% over SAMSEG, and 43.5% ± 7.1% over WMH-SynthSeg across four diverse test datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication elaborates the contribution by providing concrete quantitative validation and comparative performance, clarifying the tool's strengths.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of segcsvd WMH demonstrates significantly enhanced accuracy and robustness compared to three widely used WMH segmentation tools. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: segcsvd_WMH demonstrated superior accuracy, achieving mean Dice score improvements of 7.8% ± 9.7% over HyperMapp3r, 21.8% ± 8.6% over SAMSEG, and 43.5% ± 7.1% over WMH-SynthSeg across four diverse test datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies and specifies the claimed accuracy and robustness improvements, providing clearer evidence for the preprint's performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Guidelines for the integration of AI in medicine generally excel in scope, purpose, and editorial independence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Guidelines generally excel in scope, purpose, and editorial independence, but there is significant variability in applicability and the rigor of guideline development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the original positive assessment but adds nuance about variability in applicability and rigor, clarifying the initial statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant variability in the applicability and rigor of guideline development for AI in medicine. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Guidelines generally excel in scope, purpose, and editorial independence, but there is significant variability in applicability and the rigor of guideline development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates this variability claim directly within the broader summary statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Well-established initiatives such as DECIDE-AI, SPIRIT-AI, and CONSORT-AI have demonstrated high quality, particularly in terms of stakeholder involvement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Well-established initiatives such as TRIPOD+AI, DECIDE-AI, SPIRIT-AI, and CONSORT-AI have shown high quality, particularly in terms of stakeholder involvement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same assessment but expands the set of named initiatives (adding TRIPOD+AI), clarifying and slightly broadening the examples.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reproducibility, ethical, and environmental aspects of AI in medicine require further attention from both medical and AI communities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The reproducibility, ethical, and environmental aspects of AI in medicine still need attention from both medical and AI communities. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same limitation/need for attention with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a crucial need for high-quality guidelines that adhere to the principles of Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability and Reusability (FAIR). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need for the development of integrated and comprehensive reporting guidelines that adhere to the principles of Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability and Reusability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the call for guidelines adhering to FAIR principles with the same intent and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Histopathological evaluation of prostate biopsies using the Gleason scoring system is critical for prostate cancer diagnosis and treatment selection. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this specific clinical role of Gleason scoring; it focuses on AI tools and their impacts rather than repeating the diagnostic centrality claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Studies have demonstrated AI’s capability to diagnose and grade prostate cancer comparably to expert pathologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Studies have demonstrated AI's capability to diagnose and grade prostate cancer comparably to expert pathologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication directly restates the preprint result that AI can match expert pathologists in diagnosis and grading.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The primary objective of the study is to develop a high-performing and robust AI model for diagnosis and Gleason scoring of prostate cancer in core needle biopsies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The primary objective of the study is to develop a high-performing and robust AI model for diagnosis and Gleason scoring of prostate cancer. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same primary objective; the wording is essentially identical and intent matches.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External validations on diverse data sets have been limited and often show reduced performance. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: External validations of AI systems on diverse data sets have shown reduced performance. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the limitation about reduced performance on external diverse datasets with the same meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The protocol adheres to TRIPOD+AI, PIECES, CLAIM, and other relevant best practices. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The protocol adheres to best practices for AI study designs and validation methods. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms adherence to best practices but summarizes the specific named standards into a general statement, clarifying rather than contradicting the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI risk prediction model demonstrated an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and was capable of categorising patients into high and low risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and was capable of categorising patients into high and low risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is effectively identical between preprint and publication; the claim content, scope and certainty are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model’s sensitivity was 70% for identifying high-risk patients and had a negative predictive value of 96%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model's sensitivity was 70% for identifying high-risk patients and had a negative predictive value of 96%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical performance metrics and wording are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI can support more streamlined and efficient preoperative risk stratification, potentially reducing the burden on preoperative assessment teams. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI can support more streamlined and efficient preoperative risk stratification, potentially reducing the burden on preoperative assessment teams. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language is preserved verbatim, with no change in scope or expressed certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model offers a sophisticated adjunct to clinical decision-making around determining risk. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model offers a sophisticated adjunct to clinical decision-making around determining risk. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim about the model as an adjunct to decision-making is unchanged in wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sleep quality could predict individual depressive symptoms severity (DSS) at the individual subject level across three cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sleep quality could predict individual depressive symptoms severity (DSS) with a correlation of r = 0.43. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies the predictive association (r=0.43), making the original claim more explicit and providing stronger evidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Anxiety symptoms improved the performance of the predictive model and mediated the link between sleep quality and DSS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Adding anxiety to the predictive model strengthened its performance, resulting in a correlation of r = 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication gives a quantified improvement (r=0.67) for adding anxiety, clarifying and strengthening the performance aspect of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain structure and function did not play an important role in the association between sleep quality and DSS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anxiety scores mediated the association between sleep quality and DSS, contrary to brain measurements. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the finding as mediation by anxiety contrasted with brain measurements; the core negative role for brain measures is preserved but expressed with lower certainty and framed as a contrast.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive model demonstrated robust results in out-of-cohort validations in other cross-sectional datasets and a longitudinal sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Out-of-cohort validations provided robust similar results for the predictive role of sleep quality on DSS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same robust out-of-cohort validation results and maintains the claim's scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Posts discussing COVID-19 and related comorbid disorders spiked in early- to mid-March around the time of global implementation of lockdowns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Posts discussing COVID-19 and related comorbid disorders spiked in early March to mid-March around the time of global implementation of lockdowns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Timing and content of the spike are reported identically in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pandemic and corresponding public response has had a significant impact on posters' queries regarding mental health. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pandemic and corresponding public response has had a significant impact on posters' queries regarding mental health. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is preserved but the publication presents it with lower stated certainty (medium vs high), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: It is feasible to characterise the content of online health forum user posts regarding COVID-19 and measure changes over time. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A total of 35 581 posts (4.8%) contained a COVID-19 keyword. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete counts that demonstrate feasibility; the claim is thereby clarified with empirical evidence rather than remaining a methodological statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Social media data sources such as online health forums can be harnessed to strengthen population-level mental health surveillance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Social media data sources such as online health forums can be harnessed to strengthen population-level mental health surveillance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The policy/conclusion about harnessing social media for surveillance is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SGD users engaged more frequently in discussions related to 'friends and family' and 'wear masks' compared to non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SGD users engaged more frequently in discussions related to 'friends and family' and 'wear masks' compared to non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical reporting of topics with the same comparative claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SGD users exhibited a significantly higher proportion of positive sentiment in tweets about vaccines compared to non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SGD users exhibited a marked higher proportion of positive sentiment in tweets about vaccines compared to non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sentiment comparison is preserved with equivalent emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SGD users disclosed significantly higher frequencies of mentioning 61 out of 69 COVID-related symptoms compared to non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SGD users disclosed significantly higher frequencies of mentioning COVID-related symptoms than non-SGD users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the comparison but removes the specific '61 out of 69' detail, representing a revision in specificity while keeping the overall finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results provide insights into the unique needs and experiences of the SGD community during the pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide insights into the unique needs and experiences of the SGD community during the pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language is preserved verbatim and retains the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeTraC demonstrates high accuracy of 95.12% in the detection of COVID-19 X-ray images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeTraC achieved a high accuracy of 93.1% with a sensitivity of 100% in detecting COVID-19 X-ray images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different numerical accuracy (93.1% vs 95.12%) and adjusts sensitivity, so the performance claim has been revised and overall performance is modestly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeTraC achieves a sensitivity of 97.91% and a specificity of 91.87% in detecting COVID-19 cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeTraC achieved a high accuracy of 93.1% with a sensitivity of 100% in detecting COVID-19 X-ray images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Reported sensitivity and specificity differ between versions (publication reports sensitivity=100% and omits the prior specificity), indicating a revision of the numerical performance details.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification of medical images remains a significant challenge due to the limited availability of annotated medical images. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification of medical images remains a significant challenge due to the limited availability of annotated medical images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The statement is preserved but presented with higher certainty in the publication, so the claim's confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transfer learning provides a promising solution for adapting knowledge from generic object recognition tasks to medical image classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep CNN called Decompose, Transfer, and Compose (DeTraC) effectively classifies COVID-19 chest X-ray images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The general preprint claim about transfer learning is made concrete in the publication by demonstrating an effective transfer-learning-based model (DeTraC); the concept is clarified and reported with higher certainty.
